112 def get_reader(Reader=None, Inputter=None, Outputter=None, **kwargs):
113     """
114     Initialize a table reader allowing for common customizations.  Most of the
115     default behavior for various parameters is determined by the Reader class.
116 
117     Parameters
118     ----------
119     Reader : `~astropy.io.ascii.BaseReader`
120         Reader class (DEPRECATED). Default is :class:`Basic`.
121     Inputter : `~astropy.io.ascii.BaseInputter`
122         Inputter class
123     Outputter : `~astropy.io.ascii.BaseOutputter`
124         Outputter class
125     delimiter : str
126         Column delimiter string
127     comment : str
128         Regular expression defining a comment line in table
129     quotechar : str
130         One-character string to quote fields containing special characters
131     header_start : int
132         Line index for the header line not counting comment or blank lines.
133         A line with only whitespace is considered blank.
134     data_start : int
135         Line index for the start of data not counting comment or blank lines.
136         A line with only whitespace is considered blank.
137     data_end : int
138         Line index for the end of data not counting comment or blank lines.
139         This value can be negative to count from the end.
140     converters : dict
141         Dict of converters.
142     data_Splitter : `~astropy.io.ascii.BaseSplitter`
143         Splitter class to split data columns.
144     header_Splitter : `~astropy.io.ascii.BaseSplitter`
145         Splitter class to split header columns.
146     names : list
147         List of names corresponding to each data column.
148     include_names : list, optional
149         List of names to include in output.
150     exclude_names : list
151         List of names to exclude from output (applied after ``include_names``).
152     fill_values : tuple, list of tuple
153         Specification of fill values for bad or missing table values.
154     fill_include_names : list
155         List of names to include in fill_values.
156     fill_exclude_names : list
157         List of names to exclude from fill_values (applied after ``fill_include_names``).
158 
159     Returns
160     -------
161     reader : `~astropy.io.ascii.BaseReader` subclass
162         ASCII format reader instance
163     """
164     # This function is a light wrapper around core._get_reader to provide a
165     # public interface with a default Reader.
166     if Reader is None:
167         # Default reader is Basic unless fast reader is forced
168         fast_reader = _get_fast_reader_dict(kwargs)
169         if fast_reader['enable'] == 'force':
170             Reader = fastbasic.FastBasic
171         else:
172             Reader = basic.Basic
173 
174     reader = core._get_reader(Reader, Inputter=Inputter, Outputter=Outputter, **kwargs)
175     return reader
176 
177 
178 def _get_format_class(format, ReaderWriter, label):
179     if format is not None and ReaderWriter is not None:
180         raise ValueError(f'Cannot supply both format and {label} keywords')
181 
182     if format is not None:
183         if format in core.FORMAT_CLASSES:
184             ReaderWriter = core.FORMAT_CLASSES[format]
185         else:
186             raise ValueError('ASCII format {!r} not in allowed list {}'
187                              .format(format, sorted(core.FORMAT_CLASSES)))
188     return ReaderWriter
189 
190 
191 def _get_fast_reader_dict(kwargs):
192     """Convert 'fast_reader' key in kwargs into a dict if not already and make sure
193     'enable' key is available.
194     """
195     fast_reader = copy.deepcopy(kwargs.get('fast_reader', True))
196     if isinstance(fast_reader, dict):
197         fast_reader.setdefault('enable', 'force')
198     else:
199         fast_reader = {'enable': fast_reader}
200     return fast_reader
201 
202 
203 def _validate_read_write_kwargs(read_write, **kwargs):
204     """Validate types of keyword arg inputs to read() or write()."""
205 
206     def is_ducktype(val, cls):
207         """Check if ``val`` is an instance of ``cls`` or "seems" like one:
208         ``cls(val) == val`` does not raise and exception and is `True`. In
209         this way you can pass in ``np.int16(2)`` and have that count as `int`.
210 
211         This has a special-case of ``cls`` being 'list-like', meaning it is
212         an iterable but not a string.
213         """
214         if cls == 'list-like':
215             ok = (not isinstance(val, str)
216                   and isinstance(val, collections.abc.Iterable))
217         else:
218             ok = isinstance(val, cls)
219             if not ok:
220                 # See if ``val`` walks and quacks like a ``cls```.
221                 try:
222                     new_val = cls(val)
223                     assert new_val == val
224                 except Exception:
225                     ok = False
226                 else:
227                     ok = True
228         return ok
229 
230     kwarg_types = READ_KWARG_TYPES if read_write == 'read' else WRITE_KWARG_TYPES
231 
232     for arg, val in kwargs.items():
233         # Kwarg type checking is opt-in, so kwargs not in the list are considered OK.
234         # This reflects that some readers allow additional arguments that may not
235         # be well-specified, e.g. ```__init__(self, **kwargs)`` is an option.
236         if arg not in kwarg_types or val is None:
237             continue
238 
239         # Single type or tuple of types for this arg (like isinstance())
240         types = kwarg_types[arg]
241         err_msg = (f"{read_write}() argument '{arg}' must be a "
242                    f"{types} object, got {type(val)} instead")
243 
244         # Force `types` to be a tuple for the any() check below
245         if not isinstance(types, tuple):
246             types = (types,)
247 
248         if not any(is_ducktype(val, cls) for cls in types):
249             raise TypeError(err_msg)
250 
251 
252 def read(table, guess=None, **kwargs):
253     # This the final output from reading. Static analysis indicates the reading
254     # logic (which is indeed complex) might not define `dat`, thus do so here.
255     dat = None
256 
257     # Docstring defined below
258     del _read_trace[:]
259 
260     # Downstream readers might munge kwargs
261     kwargs = copy.deepcopy(kwargs)
262 
263     _validate_read_write_kwargs('read', **kwargs)
264 
265     # Convert 'fast_reader' key in kwargs into a dict if not already and make sure
266     # 'enable' key is available.
267     fast_reader = _get_fast_reader_dict(kwargs)
268     kwargs['fast_reader'] = fast_reader
269 
270     if fast_reader['enable'] and fast_reader.get('chunk_size'):
271         return _read_in_chunks(table, **kwargs)
272 
273     if 'fill_values' not in kwargs:
274         kwargs['fill_values'] = [('', '0')]
275 
276     # If an Outputter is supplied in kwargs that will take precedence.
277     if 'Outputter' in kwargs:  # user specified Outputter, not supported for fast reading
278         fast_reader['enable'] = False
279 
280     format = kwargs.get('format')
281     # Dictionary arguments are passed by reference per default and thus need
282     # special protection:
283     new_kwargs = copy.deepcopy(kwargs)
284     kwargs['fast_reader'] = copy.deepcopy(fast_reader)
285 
286     # Get the Reader class based on possible format and Reader kwarg inputs.
287     Reader = _get_format_class(format, kwargs.get('Reader'), 'Reader')
288     if Reader is not None:
289         new_kwargs['Reader'] = Reader
290         format = Reader._format_name
291 
292     # Remove format keyword if there, this is only allowed in read() not get_reader()
293     if 'format' in new_kwargs:
294         del new_kwargs['format']
295 
296     if guess is None:
297         guess = _GUESS
298 
299     if guess:
300         # If ``table`` is probably an HTML file then tell guess function to add
301         # the HTML reader at the top of the guess list.  This is in response to
302         # issue #3691 (and others) where libxml can segfault on a long non-HTML
303         # file, thus prompting removal of the HTML reader from the default
304         # guess list.
305         new_kwargs['guess_html'] = _probably_html(table)
306 
307         # If `table` is a filename or readable file object then read in the
308         # file now.  This prevents problems in Python 3 with the file object
309         # getting closed or left at the file end.  See #3132, #3013, #3109,
310         # #2001.  If a `readme` arg was passed that implies CDS format, in
311         # which case the original `table` as the data filename must be left
312         # intact.
313         if 'readme' not in new_kwargs:
314             encoding = kwargs.get('encoding')
315             try:
316                 with get_readable_fileobj(table, encoding=encoding) as fileobj:
317                     table = fileobj.read()
318             except ValueError:  # unreadable or invalid binary file
319                 raise
320             except Exception:
321                 pass
322             else:
323                 # Ensure that `table` has at least one \r or \n in it
324                 # so that the core.BaseInputter test of
325                 # ('\n' not in table and '\r' not in table)
326                 # will fail and so `table` cannot be interpreted there
327                 # as a filename.  See #4160.
328                 if not re.search(r'[\r\n]', table):
329                     table = table + os.linesep
330 
331                 # If the table got successfully read then look at the content
332                 # to see if is probably HTML, but only if it wasn't already
333                 # identified as HTML based on the filename.
334                 if not new_kwargs['guess_html']:
335                     new_kwargs['guess_html'] = _probably_html(table)
336 
337         # Get the table from guess in ``dat``.  If ``dat`` comes back as None
338         # then there was just one set of kwargs in the guess list so fall
339         # through below to the non-guess way so that any problems result in a
340         # more useful traceback.
341         dat = _guess(table, new_kwargs, format, fast_reader)
342         if dat is None:
343             guess = False
344 
345     if not guess:
346         if format is None:
347             reader = get_reader(**new_kwargs)
348             format = reader._format_name
349 
350         # Try the fast reader version of `format` first if applicable.  Note that
351         # if user specified a fast format (e.g. format='fast_basic') this test
352         # will fail and the else-clause below will be used.
353         if fast_reader['enable'] and f'fast_{format}' in core.FAST_CLASSES:
354             fast_kwargs = copy.deepcopy(new_kwargs)
355             fast_kwargs['Reader'] = core.FAST_CLASSES[f'fast_{format}']
356             fast_reader_rdr = get_reader(**fast_kwargs)
357             try:
358                 dat = fast_reader_rdr.read(table)
359                 _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(fast_kwargs),
360                                     'Reader': fast_reader_rdr.__class__,
361                                     'status': 'Success with fast reader (no guessing)'})
362             except (core.ParameterError, cparser.CParserError, UnicodeEncodeError) as err:
363                 # special testing value to avoid falling back on the slow reader
364                 if fast_reader['enable'] == 'force':
365                     raise core.InconsistentTableError(
366                         f'fast reader {fast_reader_rdr.__class__} exception: {err}')
367                 # If the fast reader doesn't work, try the slow version
368                 reader = get_reader(**new_kwargs)
369                 dat = reader.read(table)
370                 _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(new_kwargs),
371                                     'Reader': reader.__class__,
372                                     'status': 'Success with slow reader after failing'
373                                     ' with fast (no guessing)'})
374         else:
375             reader = get_reader(**new_kwargs)
376             dat = reader.read(table)
377             _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(new_kwargs),
378                                 'Reader': reader.__class__,
379                                 'status': 'Success with specified Reader class '
380                                           '(no guessing)'})
381 
382     # Static analysis (pyright) indicates `dat` might be left undefined, so just
383     # to be sure define it at the beginning and check here.
384     if dat is None:
385         raise RuntimeError('read() function failed due to code logic error, '
386                            'please report this bug on github')
387 
388     return dat
389 
390 
391 read.__doc__ = core.READ_DOCSTRING
392 
393 
394 def _guess(table, read_kwargs, format, fast_reader):
395     """
396     Try to read the table using various sets of keyword args.  Start with the
397     standard guess list and filter to make it unique and consistent with
398     user-supplied read keyword args.  Finally, if none of those work then
399     try the original user-supplied keyword args.
400 
401     Parameters
402     ----------
403     table : str, file-like, list
404         Input table as a file name, file-like object, list of strings, or
405         single newline-separated string.
406     read_kwargs : dict
407         Keyword arguments from user to be supplied to reader
408     format : str
409         Table format
410     fast_reader : dict
411         Options for the C engine fast reader.  See read() function for details.
412 
413     Returns
414     -------
415     dat : `~astropy.table.Table` or None
416         Output table or None if only one guess format was available
417     """
418 
419     # Keep a trace of all failed guesses kwarg
420     failed_kwargs = []
421 
422     # Get an ordered list of read() keyword arg dicts that will be cycled
423     # through in order to guess the format.
424     full_list_guess = _get_guess_kwargs_list(read_kwargs)
425 
426     # If a fast version of the reader is available, try that before the slow version
427     if (fast_reader['enable'] and format is not None and f'fast_{format}' in
428             core.FAST_CLASSES):
429         fast_kwargs = copy.deepcopy(read_kwargs)
430         fast_kwargs['Reader'] = core.FAST_CLASSES[f'fast_{format}']
431         full_list_guess = [fast_kwargs] + full_list_guess
432     else:
433         fast_kwargs = None
434 
435     # Filter the full guess list so that each entry is consistent with user kwarg inputs.
436     # This also removes any duplicates from the list.
437     filtered_guess_kwargs = []
438     fast_reader = read_kwargs.get('fast_reader')
439 
440     for guess_kwargs in full_list_guess:
441         # If user specified slow reader then skip all fast readers
442         if (fast_reader['enable'] is False
443                 and guess_kwargs['Reader'] in core.FAST_CLASSES.values()):
444             _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(guess_kwargs),
445                                 'Reader': guess_kwargs['Reader'].__class__,
446                                 'status': 'Disabled: reader only available in fast version',
447                                 'dt': f'{0.0:.3f} ms'})
448             continue
449 
450         # If user required a fast reader then skip all non-fast readers
451         if (fast_reader['enable'] == 'force'
452                 and guess_kwargs['Reader'] not in core.FAST_CLASSES.values()):
453             _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(guess_kwargs),
454                                 'Reader': guess_kwargs['Reader'].__class__,
455                                 'status': 'Disabled: no fast version of reader available',
456                                 'dt': f'{0.0:.3f} ms'})
457             continue
458 
459         guess_kwargs_ok = True  # guess_kwargs are consistent with user_kwargs?
460         for key, val in read_kwargs.items():
461             # Do guess_kwargs.update(read_kwargs) except that if guess_args has
462             # a conflicting key/val pair then skip this guess entirely.
463             if key not in guess_kwargs:
464                 guess_kwargs[key] = copy.deepcopy(val)
465             elif val != guess_kwargs[key] and guess_kwargs != fast_kwargs:
466                 guess_kwargs_ok = False
467                 break
468 
469         if not guess_kwargs_ok:
470             # User-supplied kwarg is inconsistent with the guess-supplied kwarg, e.g.
471             # user supplies delimiter="|" but the guess wants to try delimiter=" ",
472             # so skip the guess entirely.
473             continue
474 
475         # Add the guess_kwargs to filtered list only if it is not already there.
476         if guess_kwargs not in filtered_guess_kwargs:
477             filtered_guess_kwargs.append(guess_kwargs)
478 
479     # If there are not at least two formats to guess then return no table
480     # (None) to indicate that guessing did not occur.  In that case the
481     # non-guess read() will occur and any problems will result in a more useful
482     # traceback.
483     if len(filtered_guess_kwargs) <= 1:
484         return None
485 
486     # Define whitelist of exceptions that are expected from readers when
487     # processing invalid inputs.  Note that OSError must fall through here
488     # so one cannot simply catch any exception.
489     guess_exception_classes = (core.InconsistentTableError, ValueError, TypeError,
490                                AttributeError, core.OptionalTableImportError,
491                                core.ParameterError, cparser.CParserError)
492 
493     # Now cycle through each possible reader and associated keyword arguments.
494     # Try to read the table using those args, and if an exception occurs then
495     # keep track of the failed guess and move on.
496     for guess_kwargs in filtered_guess_kwargs:
497         t0 = time.time()
498         try:
499             # If guessing will try all Readers then use strict req'ts on column names
500             if 'Reader' not in read_kwargs:
501                 guess_kwargs['strict_names'] = True
502 
503             reader = get_reader(**guess_kwargs)
504 
505             reader.guessing = True
506             dat = reader.read(table)
507             _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(guess_kwargs),
508                                 'Reader': reader.__class__,
509                                 'status': 'Success (guessing)',
510                                 'dt': f'{(time.time() - t0) * 1000:.3f} ms'})
511             return dat
512 
513         except guess_exception_classes as err:
514             _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(guess_kwargs),
515                                 'status': f'{err.__class__.__name__}: {str(err)}',
516                                 'dt': f'{(time.time() - t0) * 1000:.3f} ms'})
517             failed_kwargs.append(guess_kwargs)
518     else:
519         # Failed all guesses, try the original read_kwargs without column requirements
520         try:
521             reader = get_reader(**read_kwargs)
522             dat = reader.read(table)
523             _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(read_kwargs),
524                                 'Reader': reader.__class__,
525                                 'status': 'Success with original kwargs without strict_names '
526                                           '(guessing)'})
527             return dat
528 
529         except guess_exception_classes as err:
530             _read_trace.append({'kwargs': copy.deepcopy(read_kwargs),
531                                 'status': f'{err.__class__.__name__}: {str(err)}'})
532             failed_kwargs.append(read_kwargs)
533             lines = ['\nERROR: Unable to guess table format with the guesses listed below:']
534             for kwargs in failed_kwargs:
535                 sorted_keys = sorted([x for x in sorted(kwargs)
536                                       if x not in ('Reader', 'Outputter')])
537                 reader_repr = repr(kwargs.get('Reader', basic.Basic))
538                 keys_vals = ['Reader:' + re.search(r"\.(\w+)'>", reader_repr).group(1)]
539                 kwargs_sorted = ((key, kwargs[key]) for key in sorted_keys)
540                 keys_vals.extend([f'{key}: {val!r}' for key, val in kwargs_sorted])
541                 lines.append(' '.join(keys_vals))
542 
543             msg = ['',
544                    '************************************************************************',
545                    '** ERROR: Unable to guess table format with the guesses listed above. **',
546                    '**                                                                    **',
547                    '** To figure out why the table did not read, use guess=False and      **',
548                    '** fast_reader=False, along with any appropriate arguments to read(). **',
549                    '** In particular specify the format and any known attributes like the **',
550                    '** delimiter.                                                         **',
551                    '************************************************************************']
552             lines.extend(msg)
553             raise core.InconsistentTableError('\n'.join(lines))
554 
555 
556 def _get_guess_kwargs_list(read_kwargs):
557     """
558     Get the full list of reader keyword argument dicts that are the basis
559     for the format guessing process.  The returned full list will then be:
560 
561     - Filtered to be consistent with user-supplied kwargs
562     - Cleaned to have only unique entries
563     - Used one by one to try reading the input table
564 
565     Note that the order of the guess list has been tuned over years of usage.
566     Maintainers need to be very careful about any adjustments as the
567     reasoning may not be immediately evident in all cases.
568 
569     This list can (and usually does) include duplicates.  This is a result
570     of the order tuning, but these duplicates get removed later.
571 
572     Parameters
573     ----------
574     read_kwargs : dict
575         User-supplied read keyword args
576 
577     Returns
578     -------
579     guess_kwargs_list : list
580         List of read format keyword arg dicts
581     """
582     guess_kwargs_list = []
583 
584     # If the table is probably HTML based on some heuristics then start with the
585     # HTML reader.
586     if read_kwargs.pop('guess_html', None):
587         guess_kwargs_list.append(dict(Reader=html.HTML))
588 
589     # Start with ECSV because an ECSV file will be read by Basic.  This format
590     # has very specific header requirements and fails out quickly.
591     guess_kwargs_list.append(dict(Reader=ecsv.Ecsv))
592 
593     # Now try readers that accept the user-supplied keyword arguments
594     # (actually include all here - check for compatibility of arguments later).
595     # FixedWidthTwoLine would also be read by Basic, so it needs to come first;
596     # same for RST.
597     for reader in (fixedwidth.FixedWidthTwoLine, rst.RST,
598                    fastbasic.FastBasic, basic.Basic,
599                    fastbasic.FastRdb, basic.Rdb,
600                    fastbasic.FastTab, basic.Tab,
601                    cds.Cds, mrt.Mrt, daophot.Daophot, sextractor.SExtractor,
602                    ipac.Ipac, latex.Latex, latex.AASTex):
603         guess_kwargs_list.append(dict(Reader=reader))
604 
605     # Cycle through the basic-style readers using all combinations of delimiter
606     # and quotechar.
607     for Reader in (fastbasic.FastCommentedHeader, basic.CommentedHeader,
608                    fastbasic.FastBasic, basic.Basic,
609                    fastbasic.FastNoHeader, basic.NoHeader):
610         for delimiter in ("|", ",", " ", r"\s"):
611             for quotechar in ('"', "'"):
612                 guess_kwargs_list.append(dict(
613                     Reader=Reader, delimiter=delimiter, quotechar=quotechar))
614 
615     return guess_kwargs_list
616 
617 
618 def _read_in_chunks(table, **kwargs):
619     """
620     For fast_reader read the ``table`` in chunks and vstack to create
621     a single table, OR return a generator of chunk tables.
622     """
623     fast_reader = kwargs['fast_reader']
624     chunk_size = fast_reader.pop('chunk_size')
625     chunk_generator = fast_reader.pop('chunk_generator', False)
626     fast_reader['parallel'] = False  # No parallel with chunks
627 
628     tbl_chunks = _read_in_chunks_generator(table, chunk_size, **kwargs)
629     if chunk_generator:
630         return tbl_chunks
631 
632     tbl0 = next(tbl_chunks)
633     masked = tbl0.masked
634 
635     # Numpy won't allow resizing the original so make a copy here.
636     out_cols = {col.name: col.data.copy() for col in tbl0.itercols()}
637 
638     str_kinds = ('S', 'U')
639     for tbl in tbl_chunks:
640         masked |= tbl.masked
641         for name, col in tbl.columns.items():
642             # Concatenate current column data and new column data
643 
644             # If one of the inputs is string-like and the other is not, then
645             # convert the non-string to a string.  In a perfect world this would
646             # be handled by numpy, but as of numpy 1.13 this results in a string
647             # dtype that is too long (https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/10062).
648 
649             col1, col2 = out_cols[name], col.data
650             if col1.dtype.kind in str_kinds and col2.dtype.kind not in str_kinds:
651                 col2 = np.array(col2.tolist(), dtype=col1.dtype.kind)
652             elif col2.dtype.kind in str_kinds and col1.dtype.kind not in str_kinds:
653                 col1 = np.array(col1.tolist(), dtype=col2.dtype.kind)
654 
655             # Choose either masked or normal concatenation
656             concatenate = np.ma.concatenate if masked else np.concatenate
657 
658             out_cols[name] = concatenate([col1, col2])
659 
660     # Make final table from numpy arrays, converting dict to list
661     out_cols = [out_cols[name] for name in tbl0.colnames]
662     out = tbl0.__class__(out_cols, names=tbl0.colnames, meta=tbl0.meta,
663                          copy=False)
664 
665     return out
666 
667 
668 def _read_in_chunks_generator(table, chunk_size, **kwargs):
669     """
670     For fast_reader read the ``table`` in chunks and return a generator
671     of tables for each chunk.
672     """
673 
674     @contextlib.contextmanager
675     def passthrough_fileobj(fileobj, encoding=None):
676         """Stub for get_readable_fileobj, which does not seem to work in Py3
677         for input file-like object, see #6460"""
678         yield fileobj
679 
680     # Set up to coerce `table` input into a readable file object by selecting
681     # an appropriate function.
682 
683     # Convert table-as-string to a File object.  Finding a newline implies
684     # that the string is not a filename.
685     if (isinstance(table, str) and ('\n' in table or '\r' in table)):
686         table = StringIO(table)
687         fileobj_context = passthrough_fileobj
688     elif hasattr(table, 'read') and hasattr(table, 'seek'):
689         fileobj_context = passthrough_fileobj
690     else:
691         # string filename or pathlib
692         fileobj_context = get_readable_fileobj
693 
694     # Set up for iterating over chunks
695     kwargs['fast_reader']['return_header_chars'] = True
696     header = ''  # Table header (up to start of data)
697     prev_chunk_chars = ''  # Chars from previous chunk after last newline
698     first_chunk = True  # True for the first chunk, False afterward
699 
700     with fileobj_context(table, encoding=kwargs.get('encoding')) as fh:
701 
702         while True:
703             chunk = fh.read(chunk_size)
704             # Got fewer chars than requested, must be end of file
705             final_chunk = len(chunk) < chunk_size
706 
707             # If this is the last chunk and there is only whitespace then break
708             if final_chunk and not re.search(r'\S', chunk):
709                 break
710 
711             # Step backwards from last character in chunk and find first newline
712             for idx in range(len(chunk) - 1, -1, -1):
713                 if final_chunk or chunk[idx] == '\n':
714                     break
715             else:
716                 raise ValueError('no newline found in chunk (chunk_size too small?)')
717 
718             # Stick on the header to the chunk part up to (and including) the
719             # last newline.  Make sure the small strings are concatenated first.
720             complete_chunk = (header + prev_chunk_chars) + chunk[:idx + 1]
721             prev_chunk_chars = chunk[idx + 1:]
722 
723             # Now read the chunk as a complete table
724             tbl = read(complete_chunk, guess=False, **kwargs)
725 
726             # For the first chunk pop the meta key which contains the header
727             # characters (everything up to the start of data) then fix kwargs
728             # so it doesn't return that in meta any more.
729             if first_chunk:
730                 header = tbl.meta.pop('__ascii_fast_reader_header_chars__')
731                 first_chunk = False
732 
733             yield tbl
734 
735             if final_chunk:
736                 break
737 
738 
739 extra_writer_pars = ('delimiter', 'comment', 'quotechar', 'formats',
740                      'names', 'include_names', 'exclude_names', 'strip_whitespace')
741 
742 
743 def get_writer(Writer=None, fast_writer=True, **kwargs):
744     """
745     Initialize a table writer allowing for common customizations.  Most of the
746     default behavior for various parameters is determined by the Writer class.
747 
748     Parameters
749     ----------
750     Writer : ``Writer``
751         Writer class (DEPRECATED). Defaults to :class:`Basic`.
752     delimiter : str
753         Column delimiter string
754     comment : str
755         String defining a comment line in table
756     quotechar : str
757         One-character string to quote fields containing special characters
758     formats : dict
759         Dictionary of format specifiers or formatting functions
760     strip_whitespace : bool
761         Strip surrounding whitespace from column values.
762     names : list
763         List of names corresponding to each data column
764     include_names : list
765         List of names to include in output.
766     exclude_names : list
767         List of names to exclude from output (applied after ``include_names``)
768     fast_writer : bool
769         Whether to use the fast Cython writer.
770 
771     Returns
772     -------
773     writer : `~astropy.io.ascii.BaseReader` subclass
774         ASCII format writer instance
775     """
776     if Writer is None:
777         Writer = basic.Basic
778     if 'strip_whitespace' not in kwargs:
779         kwargs['strip_whitespace'] = True
780     writer = core._get_writer(Writer, fast_writer, **kwargs)
781 
782     # Handle the corner case of wanting to disable writing table comments for the
783     # commented_header format.  This format *requires* a string for `write_comment`
784     # because that is used for the header column row, so it is not possible to
785     # set the input `comment` to None.  Without adding a new keyword or assuming
786     # a default comment character, there is no other option but to tell user to
787     # simply remove the meta['comments'].
788     if (isinstance(writer, (basic.CommentedHeader, fastbasic.FastCommentedHeader))
789             and not isinstance(kwargs.get('comment', ''), str)):
790         raise ValueError("for the commented_header writer you must supply a string\n"
791                          "value for the `comment` keyword.  In order to disable writing\n"
792                          "table comments use `del t.meta['comments']` prior to writing.")
793 
794     return writer
795 
796 
797 def write(table, output=None, format=None, Writer=None, fast_writer=True, *,
798           overwrite=False, **kwargs):
799     # Docstring inserted below
800 
801     _validate_read_write_kwargs('write', format=format, fast_writer=fast_writer,
802                                 overwrite=overwrite, **kwargs)
803 
804     if isinstance(output, str):
805         if not overwrite and os.path.lexists(output):
806             raise OSError(NOT_OVERWRITING_MSG.format(output))
807 
808     if output is None:
809         output = sys.stdout
810 
811     # Ensure that `table` is a Table subclass.
812     names = kwargs.get('names')
813     if isinstance(table, Table):
814         # While we are only going to read data from columns, we may need to
815         # to adjust info attributes such as format, so we make a shallow copy.
816         table = table.__class__(table, names=names, copy=False)
817     else:
818         # Otherwise, create a table from the input.
819         table = Table(table, names=names, copy=False)
820 
821     table0 = table[:0].copy()
822     core._apply_include_exclude_names(table0, kwargs.get('names'),
823                                       kwargs.get('include_names'), kwargs.get('exclude_names'))
824     diff_format_with_names = set(kwargs.get('formats', [])) - set(table0.colnames)
825 
826     if diff_format_with_names:
827         warnings.warn(
828             'The key(s) {} specified in the formats argument do not match a column name.'
829             .format(diff_format_with_names), AstropyWarning)
830 
831     if table.has_mixin_columns:
832         fast_writer = False
833 
834     Writer = _get_format_class(format, Writer, 'Writer')
835     writer = get_writer(Writer=Writer, fast_writer=fast_writer, **kwargs)
836     if writer._format_name in core.FAST_CLASSES:
837         writer.write(table, output)
838         return
839 
840     lines = writer.write(table)
841 
842     # Write the lines to output
843     outstr = os.linesep.join(lines)
844     if not hasattr(output, 'write'):
845         # NOTE: we need to specify newline='', otherwise the default
846         # behavior is for Python to translate \r\n (which we write because
847         # of os.linesep) into \r\r\n. Specifying newline='' disables any
848         # auto-translation.
849         output = open(output, 'w', newline='')
850         output.write(outstr)
851         output.write(os.linesep)
852         output.close()
853     else:
854         output.write(outstr)
855         output.write(os.linesep)
856 
857 
858 write.__doc__ = core.WRITE_DOCSTRING

58 def col_copy(col, copy_indices=True):
59     """
60     Mixin-safe version of Column.copy() (with copy_data=True).
61 
62     Parameters
63     ----------
64     col : Column or mixin column
65         Input column
66     copy_indices : bool
67         Copy the column ``indices`` attribute
68 
69     Returns
70     -------
71     col : Copy of input column
72     """
73     if isinstance(col, BaseColumn):
74         return col.copy()
75 
76     newcol = col.copy() if hasattr(col, 'copy') else deepcopy(col)
77     # If the column has info defined, we copy it and adjust any indices
78     # to point to the copied column.  By guarding with the if statement,
79     # we avoid side effects (of creating the default info instance).
80     if 'info' in col.__dict__:
81         newcol.info = col.info
82         if copy_indices and col.info.indices:
83             newcol.info.indices = deepcopy(col.info.indices)
84             for index in newcol.info.indices:
85                 index.replace_col(col, newcol)
86 
87     return newcol

90 class FalseArray(np.ndarray):
91     """
92     Boolean mask array that is always False.
93 
94     This is used to create a stub ``mask`` property which is a boolean array of
95     ``False`` used by default for mixin columns and corresponding to the mixin
96     column data shape.  The ``mask`` looks like a normal numpy array but an
97     exception will be raised if ``True`` is assigned to any element.  The
98     consequences of the limitation are most obvious in the high-level table
99     operations.
100 
101     Parameters
102     ----------
103     shape : tuple
104         Data shape
105     """
106     def __new__(cls, shape):
107         obj = np.zeros(shape, dtype=bool).view(cls)
108         return obj
109 
110     def __setitem__(self, item, val):
111         val = np.asarray(val)
112         if np.any(val):
113             raise ValueError('Cannot set any element of {} class to True'
114                              .format(self.__class__.__name__))

117 def _expand_string_array_for_values(arr, values):
118     """
119     For string-dtype return a version of ``arr`` that is wide enough for ``values``.
120     If ``arr`` is not string-dtype or does not need expansion then return ``arr``.
121 
122     Parameters
123     ----------
124     arr : np.ndarray
125         Input array
126     values : scalar or array-like
127         Values for width comparison for string arrays
128 
129     Returns
130     -------
131     arr_expanded : np.ndarray
132 
133     """
134     if arr.dtype.kind in ('U', 'S') and values is not np.ma.masked:
135         # Find the length of the longest string in the new values.
136         values_str_len = np.char.str_len(values).max()
137 
138         # Determine character repeat count of arr.dtype.  Returns a positive
139         # int or None (something like 'U0' is not possible in numpy).  If new values
140         # are longer than current then make a new (wider) version of arr.
141         arr_str_len = dtype_bytes_or_chars(arr.dtype)
142         if arr_str_len and values_str_len > arr_str_len:
143             arr_dtype = arr.dtype.byteorder + arr.dtype.kind + str(values_str_len)
144             arr = arr.astype(arr_dtype)
145 
146     return arr
147

149 def _convert_sequence_data_to_array(data, dtype=None):
150     """Convert N-d sequence-like data to ndarray or MaskedArray.
151 
152     This is the core function for converting Python lists or list of lists to a
153     numpy array. This handles embedded np.ma.masked constants in ``data`` along
154     with the special case of an homogeneous list of MaskedArray elements.
155 
156     Considerations:
157 
158     - np.ma.array is about 50 times slower than np.array for list input. This
159       function avoids using np.ma.array on list input.
160     - np.array emits a UserWarning for embedded np.ma.masked, but only for int
161       or float inputs. For those it converts to np.nan and forces float dtype.
162       For other types np.array is inconsistent, for instance converting
163       np.ma.masked to "0.0" for str types.
164     - Searching in pure Python for np.ma.masked in ``data`` is comparable in
165       speed to calling ``np.array(data)``.
166     - This function may end up making two additional copies of input ``data``.
167 
168     Parameters
169     ----------
170     data : N-d sequence
171         Input data, typically list or list of lists
172     dtype : None or dtype-like
173         Output datatype (None lets np.array choose)
174 
175     Returns
176     -------
177     np_data : np.ndarray or np.ma.MaskedArray
178 
179     """
180     np_ma_masked = np.ma.masked  # Avoid repeated lookups of this object
181 
182     # Special case of an homogeneous list of MaskedArray elements (see #8977).
183     # np.ma.masked is an instance of MaskedArray, so exclude those values.
184     if (hasattr(data, '__len__')
185         and len(data) > 0
186         and all(isinstance(val, np.ma.MaskedArray)
187                 and val is not np_ma_masked for val in data)):
188         np_data = np.ma.array(data, dtype=dtype)
189         return np_data
190 
191     # First convert data to a plain ndarray. If there are instances of np.ma.masked
192     # in the data this will issue a warning for int and float.
193     with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as warns:
194         # Ensure this warning from numpy is always enabled and that it is not
195         # converted to an error (which can happen during pytest).
196         warnings.filterwarnings('always', category=UserWarning,
197                                 message='.*converting a masked element.*')
198         # FutureWarning in numpy 1.21. See https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/11291
199         # and https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/18425.
200         warnings.filterwarnings('always', category=FutureWarning,
201                                 message='.*Promotion of numbers and bools to strings.*')
202         try:
203             np_data = np.array(data, dtype=dtype)
204         except np.ma.MaskError:
205             # Catches case of dtype=int with masked values, instead let it
206             # convert to float
207             np_data = np.array(data)
208         except Exception:
209             # Conversion failed for some reason, e.g. [2, 1*u.m] gives TypeError in Quantity.
210             # First try to interpret the data as Quantity. If that still fails then fall
211             # through to object
212             try:
213                 np_data = Quantity(data, dtype)
214             except Exception:
215                 dtype = object
216                 np_data = np.array(data, dtype=dtype)
217 
218     if np_data.ndim == 0 or (np_data.ndim > 0 and len(np_data) == 0):
219         # Implies input was a scalar or an empty list (e.g. initializing an
220         # empty table with pre-declared names and dtypes but no data).  Here we
221         # need to fall through to initializing with the original data=[].
222         return data
223 
224     # If there were no warnings and the data are int or float, then we are done.
225     # Other dtypes like string or complex can have masked values and the
226     # np.array() conversion gives the wrong answer (e.g. converting np.ma.masked
227     # to the string "0.0").
228     if len(warns) == 0 and np_data.dtype.kind in ('i', 'f'):
229         return np_data
230 
231     # Now we need to determine if there is an np.ma.masked anywhere in input data.
232 
233     # Make a statement like below to look for np.ma.masked in a nested sequence.
234     # Because np.array(data) succeeded we know that `data` has a regular N-d
235     # structure. Find ma_masked:
236     #   any(any(any(d2 is ma_masked for d2 in d1) for d1 in d0) for d0 in data)
237     # Using this eval avoids creating a copy of `data` in the more-usual case of
238     # no masked elements.
239     any_statement = 'd0 is ma_masked'
240     for ii in reversed(range(np_data.ndim)):
241         if ii == 0:
242             any_statement = f'any({any_statement} for d0 in data)'
243         elif ii == np_data.ndim - 1:
244             any_statement = f'any(d{ii} is ma_masked for d{ii} in d{ii-1})'
245         else:
246             any_statement = f'any({any_statement} for d{ii} in d{ii-1})'
247     context = {'ma_masked': np.ma.masked, 'data': data}
248     has_masked = eval(any_statement, context)
249 
250     # If there are any masks then explicitly change each one to a fill value and
251     # set a mask boolean array. If not has_masked then we're done.
252     if has_masked:
253         mask = np.zeros(np_data.shape, dtype=bool)
254         data_filled = np.array(data, dtype=object)
255 
256         # Make type-appropriate fill value based on initial conversion.
257         if np_data.dtype.kind == 'U':
258             fill = ''
259         elif np_data.dtype.kind == 'S':
260             fill = b''
261         else:
262             # Zero works for every numeric type.
263             fill = 0
264 
265         ranges = [range(dim) for dim in np_data.shape]
266         for idxs in itertools.product(*ranges):
267             val = data_filled[idxs]
268             if val is np_ma_masked:
269                 data_filled[idxs] = fill
270                 mask[idxs] = True
271             elif isinstance(val, bool) and dtype is None:
272                 # If we see a bool and dtype not specified then assume bool for
273                 # the entire array. Not perfect but in most practical cases OK.
274                 # Unfortunately numpy types [False, 0] as int, not bool (and
275                 # [False, np.ma.masked] => array([0.0, np.nan])).
276                 dtype = bool
277 
278         # If no dtype is provided then need to convert back to list so np.array
279         # does type autodetection.
280         if dtype is None:
281             data_filled = data_filled.tolist()
282 
283         # Use np.array first to convert `data` to ndarray (fast) and then make
284         # masked array from an ndarray with mask (fast) instead of from `data`.
285         np_data = np.ma.array(np.array(data_filled, dtype=dtype), mask=mask)
286 
287     return np_data
288

290 def _make_compare(oper):
291     """
292     Make Column comparison methods which encode the ``other`` object to utf-8
293     in the case of a bytestring dtype for Py3+.
294 
295     Parameters
296     ----------
297     oper : str
298         Operator name
299     """
300     swapped_oper = {'__eq__': '__eq__',
301                     '__ne__': '__ne__',
302                     '__gt__': '__lt__',
303                     '__lt__': '__gt__',
304                     '__ge__': '__le__',
305                     '__le__': '__ge__'}[oper]
306 
307     def _compare(self, other):
308         op = oper  # copy enclosed ref to allow swap below
309 
310         # Special case to work around #6838.  Other combinations work OK,
311         # see tests.test_column.test_unicode_sandwich_compare().  In this
312         # case just swap self and other.
313         #
314         # This is related to an issue in numpy that was addressed in np 1.13.
315         # However that fix does not make this problem go away, but maybe
316         # future numpy versions will do so.  NUMPY_LT_1_13 to get the
317         # attention of future maintainers to check (by deleting or versioning
318         # the if block below).  See #6899 discussion.
319         # 2019-06-21: still needed with numpy 1.16.
320         if (isinstance(self, MaskedColumn) and self.dtype.kind == 'U'
321                 and isinstance(other, MaskedColumn) and other.dtype.kind == 'S'):
322             self, other = other, self
323             op = swapped_oper
324 
325         if self.dtype.char == 'S':
326             other = self._encode_str(other)
327 
328         # Now just let the regular ndarray.__eq__, etc., take over.
329         result = getattr(super(Column, self), op)(other)
330         # But we should not return Column instances for this case.
331         return result.data if isinstance(result, Column) else result
332 
333     return _compare

336 class ColumnInfo(BaseColumnInfo):
337     """
338     Container for meta information like name, description, format.
339 
340     This is required when the object is used as a mixin column within a table,
341     but can be used as a general way to store meta information.
342     """
343     attrs_from_parent = BaseColumnInfo.attr_names
344     _supports_indexing = True
345     # For structured columns, data is used to store a dict of columns.
346     # Store entries in that dict as name.key instead of name.data.key.
347     _represent_as_dict_primary_data = 'data'
348 
349     def _represent_as_dict(self):
350         result = super()._represent_as_dict()
351         names = self._parent.dtype.names
352         # For a regular column, we are done, but for a structured
353         # column, we use a SerializedColumns to store the pieces.
354         if names is None:
355             return result
356 
357         from .serialize import SerializedColumn
358 
359         data = SerializedColumn()
360         # If this column has a StructuredUnit, we split it and store
361         # it on the corresponding part. Otherwise, we just store it
362         # as an attribute below.  All other attributes we remove from
363         # the parts, so that we do not store them multiple times.
364         # (Note that attributes are not linked to the parent, so it
365         # is safe to reset them.)
366         # TODO: deal with (some of) this in Column.__getitem__?
367         # Alternatively: should we store info on the first part?
368         # TODO: special-case format somehow? Can we have good formats
369         # for structured columns?
370         unit = self.unit
371         if isinstance(unit, StructuredUnit) and len(unit) == len(names):
372             units = unit.values()
373             unit = None  # No need to store as an attribute as well.
374         else:
375             units = [None] * len(names)
376         for name, part_unit in zip(names, units):
377             part = self._parent[name]
378             part.unit = part_unit
379             part.description = None
380             part.meta = {}
381             part.format = None
382             data[name] = part
383 
384         # Create the attributes required to reconstruct the column.
385         result['data'] = data
386         # Store the shape if needed. Just like scalar data, a structured data
387         # column (e.g. with dtype `f8,i8`) can be multidimensional within each
388         # row and have a shape, and that needs to be distinguished from the
389         # case that each entry in the structure has the same shape (e.g.,
390         # distinguist a column with dtype='f8,i8' and 2 elements per row from
391         # one with dtype '2f8,2i8' and just one element per row).
392         if shape := self._parent.shape[1:]:
393             result['shape'] = list(shape)
394         # Also store the standard info attributes since these are
395         # stored on the parent and can thus just be passed on as
396         # arguments.  TODO: factor out with essentially the same
397         # code in serialize._represent_mixin_as_column.
398         if unit is not None and unit != '':
399             result['unit'] = unit
400         if self.format is not None:
401             result['format'] = self.format
402         if self.description is not None:
403             result['description'] = self.description
404         if self.meta:
405             result['meta'] = self.meta
406 
407         return result
408 
409     def _construct_from_dict(self, map):
410         if not isinstance(map.get('data'), dict):
411             return super()._construct_from_dict(map)
412 
413         # Reconstruct a structured Column, by first making an empty column
414         # and then filling it with the structured data.
415         data = map.pop('data')
416         shape = tuple(map.pop('shape', ()))
417         # There are three elements in the shape of `part`:
418         # (table length, shape of structured column, shape of part like '3f8')
419         # The column `shape` only includes the second, so by adding one to its
420         # length to include the table length, we pick off a possible last bit.
421         dtype = np.dtype([(name, part.dtype, part.shape[len(shape)+1:])
422                           for name, part in data.items()])
423         units = tuple(col.info.unit for col in data.values())
424         if all(unit is not None for unit in units):
425             map['unit'] = StructuredUnit(units, dtype)
426         map.update(dtype=dtype, shape=shape, length=len(data[dtype.names[0]]))
427         # Construct the empty column from `map` (note: 'data' removed above).
428         result = super()._construct_from_dict(map)
429         # Fill it with the structured data.
430         for name in dtype.names:
431             result[name] = data[name]
432         return result
433

437         input ``cols`` and has ``length`` rows.

439         This is intended for creating an empty column object whose elements can

478 class BaseColumn(_ColumnGetitemShim, np.ndarray):
479 
480     meta = MetaData()
481 
482     def __new__(cls, data=None, name=None,
483                 dtype=None, shape=(), length=0,
484                 description=None, unit=None, format=None, meta=None,
485                 copy=False, copy_indices=True):
486         if data is None:
487             self_data = np.zeros((length,)+shape, dtype=dtype)
488         elif isinstance(data, BaseColumn) and hasattr(data, '_name'):
489             # When unpickling a MaskedColumn, ``data`` will be a bare
490             # BaseColumn with none of the expected attributes.  In this case
491             # do NOT execute this block which initializes from ``data``
492             # attributes.
493             self_data = np.array(data.data, dtype=dtype, copy=copy)
494             if description is None:
495                 description = data.description
496             if unit is None:
497                 unit = unit or data.unit
498             if format is None:
499                 format = data.format
500             if meta is None:
501                 meta = data.meta
502             if name is None:
503                 name = data.name
504         elif isinstance(data, Quantity):
505             if unit is None:
506                 self_data = np.array(data, dtype=dtype, copy=copy)
507                 unit = data.unit
508             else:
509                 self_data = Quantity(data, unit, dtype=dtype, copy=copy).value
510             # If 'info' has been defined, copy basic properties (if needed).
511             if 'info' in data.__dict__:
512                 if description is None:
513                     description = data.info.description
514                 if format is None:
515                     format = data.info.format
516                 if meta is None:
517                     meta = data.info.meta
518 
519         else:
520             if np.dtype(dtype).char == 'S':
521                 data = cls._encode_str(data)
522             self_data = np.array(data, dtype=dtype, copy=copy)
523 
524         self = self_data.view(cls)
525         self._name = None if name is None else str(name)
526         self._parent_table = None
527         self.unit = unit
528         self._format = format
529         self.description = description
530         self.meta = meta
531         self.indices = deepcopy(getattr(data, 'indices', [])) if copy_indices else []
532         for index in self.indices:
533             index.replace_col(data, self)
534 
535         return self
536 
537     @property
538     def data(self):
539         return self.view(np.ndarray)
540 
541     @property
542     def value(self):
543         return self.data
544 
545     @property
546     def parent_table(self):
547         # Note: It seems there are some cases where _parent_table is not set,
548         # such after restoring from a pickled Column.  Perhaps that should be
549         # fixed, but this is also okay for now.
550         if getattr(self, '_parent_table', None) is None:
551             return None
552         else:
553             return self._parent_table()
554 
555     @parent_table.setter
556     def parent_table(self, table):
557         if table is None:
558             self._parent_table = None
559         else:
560             self._parent_table = weakref.ref(table)
561 
562     info = ColumnInfo()

564     def copy(self, order='C', data=None, copy_data=True):
565         """
566         Return a copy of the current instance.
567 
568         If ``data`` is supplied then a view (reference) of ``data`` is used,
569         and ``copy_data`` is ignored.
570 
571         Parameters
572         ----------
573         order : {'C', 'F', 'A', 'K'}, optional
574             Controls the memory layout of the copy. 'C' means C-order,
575             'F' means F-order, 'A' means 'F' if ``a`` is Fortran contiguous,
576             'C' otherwise. 'K' means match the layout of ``a`` as closely
577             as possible. (Note that this function and :func:numpy.copy are very
578             similar, but have different default values for their order=
579             arguments.)  Default is 'C'.
580         data : array, optional
581             If supplied then use a view of ``data`` instead of the instance
582             data.  This allows copying the instance attributes and meta.
583         copy_data : bool, optional
584             Make a copy of the internal numpy array instead of using a
585             reference.  Default is True.
586 
587         Returns
588         -------
589         col : Column or MaskedColumn
590             Copy of the current column (same type as original)
591         """
592         if data is None:
593             data = self.data
594             if copy_data:
595                 data = data.copy(order)
596 
597         out = data.view(self.__class__)
598         out.__array_finalize__(self)
599 
600         # If there is meta on the original column then deepcopy (since "copy" of column
601         # implies complete independence from original).  __array_finalize__ will have already
602         # made a light copy.  I'm not sure how to avoid that initial light copy.
603         if self.meta is not None:
604             out.meta = self.meta  # MetaData descriptor does a deepcopy here
605 
606         # for MaskedColumn, MaskedArray.__array_finalize__ also copies mask
607         # from self, which is not the idea here, so undo
608         if isinstance(self, MaskedColumn):
609             out._mask = data._mask
610 
611         self._copy_groups(out)
612 
613         return out
614 
615     def __setstate__(self, state):
616         """
617         Restore the internal state of the Column/MaskedColumn for pickling
618         purposes.  This requires that the last element of ``state`` is a
619         5-tuple that has Column-specific state values.
620         """
621         # Get the Column attributes
622         names = ('_name', '_unit', '_format', 'description', 'meta', 'indices')
623         attrs = {name: val for name, val in zip(names, state[-1])}
624 
625         state = state[:-1]
626 
627         # Using super().__setstate__(state) gives
628         # "TypeError 'int' object is not iterable", raised in
629         # astropy.table._column_mixins._ColumnGetitemShim.__setstate_cython__()
630         # Previously, it seems to have given an infinite recursion.
631         # Hence, manually call the right super class to actually set up
632         # the array object.
633         super_class = ma.MaskedArray if isinstance(self, ma.MaskedArray) else np.ndarray
634         super_class.__setstate__(self, state)
635 
636         # Set the Column attributes
637         for name, val in attrs.items():
638             setattr(self, name, val)
639         self._parent_table = None
640 
641     def __reduce__(self):
642         """
643         Return a 3-tuple for pickling a Column.  Use the super-class
644         functionality but then add in a 5-tuple of Column-specific values
645         that get used in __setstate__.
646         """
647         super_class = ma.MaskedArray if isinstance(self, ma.MaskedArray) else np.ndarray
648         reconstruct_func, reconstruct_func_args, state = super_class.__reduce__(self)
649 
650         # Define Column-specific attrs and meta that gets added to state.
651         column_state = (self.name, self.unit, self.format, self.description,
652                         self.meta, self.indices)
653         state = state + (column_state,)
654 
655         return reconstruct_func, reconstruct_func_args, state
656 
657     def __array_finalize__(self, obj):
658         # Obj will be none for direct call to Column() creator
659         if obj is None:
660             return
661 
662         if callable(super().__array_finalize__):
663             super().__array_finalize__(obj)
664 
665         # Self was created from template (e.g. obj[slice] or (obj * 2))
666         # or viewcast e.g. obj.view(Column).  In either case we want to
667         # init Column attributes for self from obj if possible.
668         self.parent_table = None
669         if not hasattr(self, 'indices'):  # may have been copied in __new__
670             self.indices = []
671         self._copy_attrs(obj)
672         if 'info' in getattr(obj, '__dict__', {}):
673             self.info = obj.info
674 
675     def __array_wrap__(self, out_arr, context=None):
676         """
677         __array_wrap__ is called at the end of every ufunc.
678 
679         Normally, we want a Column object back and do not have to do anything
680         special. But there are two exceptions:
681 
682         1) If the output shape is different (e.g. for reduction ufuncs
683            like sum() or mean()), a Column still linking to a parent_table
684            makes little sense, so we return the output viewed as the
685            column content (ndarray or MaskedArray).
686            For this case, we use "[()]" to select everything, and to ensure we
687            convert a zero rank array to a scalar. (For some reason np.sum()
688            returns a zero rank scalar array while np.mean() returns a scalar;
689            So the [()] is needed for this case.
690 
691         2) When the output is created by any function that returns a boolean
692            we also want to consistently return an array rather than a column
693            (see #1446 and #1685)
694         """
695         out_arr = super().__array_wrap__(out_arr, context)
696         if (self.shape != out_arr.shape
697             or (isinstance(out_arr, BaseColumn)
698                 and (context is not None
699                      and context[0] in _comparison_functions))):
700             return out_arr.data[()]
701         else:
702             return out_arr
703

717             table = self.parent_table
718             table.columns._rename_column(self.name, val)
719

722     @property
723     def format(self):

730     @format.setter
731     def format(self, format_string):
732 
733         prev_format = getattr(self, '_format', None)
734 
735         self._format = format_string  # set new format string
736 
737         try:
738             # test whether it formats without error exemplarily
739             self.pformat(max_lines=1)
740         except Exception as err:
741             # revert to restore previous format if there was one
742             self._format = prev_format
743             raise ValueError(
744                 "Invalid format for column '{}': could not display "
745                 "values in this column using this format".format(
746                     self.name)) from err

757     def iter_str_vals(self):
758         """
759         Return an iterator that yields the string-formatted values of this
760         column.
761 
762         Returns
763         -------
764         str_vals : iterator
765             Column values formatted as strings
766         """
767         # Iterate over formatted values with no max number of lines, no column
768         # name, no unit, and ignoring the returned header info in outs.
769         _pformat_col_iter = self._formatter._pformat_col_iter
770         for str_val in _pformat_col_iter(self, -1, show_name=False, show_unit=False,
771                                          show_dtype=False, outs={}):
772             yield str_val

774     def attrs_equal(self, col):
775         """Compare the column attributes of ``col`` to this object.
776 
777         The comparison attributes are: ``name``, ``unit``, ``dtype``,
778         ``format``, ``description``, and ``meta``.
779 
780         Parameters
781         ----------
782         col : Column
783             Comparison column
784 
785         Returns
786         -------
787         equal : bool
788             True if all attributes are equal
789         """
790         if not isinstance(col, BaseColumn):
791             raise ValueError('Comparison `col` must be a Column or '
792                              'MaskedColumn object')
793 
794         attrs = ('name', 'unit', 'dtype', 'format', 'description', 'meta')
795         equal = all(getattr(self, x) == getattr(col, x) for x in attrs)
796 
797         return equal
798 
799     @property

803     def pformat(self, max_lines=None, show_name=True, show_unit=False, show_dtype=False,
804                 html=False):
805         """Return a list of formatted string representation of column values.
806 
807         If no value of ``max_lines`` is supplied then the height of the
808         screen terminal is used to set ``max_lines``.  If the terminal
809         height cannot be determined then the default will be
810         determined using the ``astropy.conf.max_lines`` configuration
811         item. If a negative value of ``max_lines`` is supplied then
812         there is no line limit applied.
813 
814         Parameters
815         ----------
816         max_lines : int
817             Maximum lines of output (header + data rows)
818 
819         show_name : bool
820             Include column name. Default is True.
821 
822         show_unit : bool
823             Include a header row for unit. Default is False.
824 
825         show_dtype : bool
826             Include column dtype. Default is False.
827 
828         html : bool
829             Format the output as an HTML table. Default is False.
830 
831         Returns
832         -------
833         lines : list
834             List of lines with header and formatted column values
835 
836         """
837         _pformat_col = self._formatter._pformat_col
838         lines, outs = _pformat_col(self, max_lines, show_name=show_name,
839                                    show_unit=show_unit, show_dtype=show_dtype,
840                                    html=html)
841         return lines
842 
843     def pprint(self, max_lines=None, show_name=True, show_unit=False, show_dtype=False):
844         """Print a formatted string representation of column values.
845 
846         If no value of ``max_lines`` is supplied then the height of the
847         screen terminal is used to set ``max_lines``.  If the terminal
848         height cannot be determined then the default will be
849         determined using the ``astropy.conf.max_lines`` configuration
850         item. If a negative value of ``max_lines`` is supplied then
851         there is no line limit applied.
852 
853         Parameters
854         ----------
855         max_lines : int
856             Maximum number of values in output
857 
858         show_name : bool
859             Include column name. Default is True.
860 
861         show_unit : bool
862             Include a header row for unit. Default is False.
863 
864         show_dtype : bool
865             Include column dtype. Default is True.
866         """
867         _pformat_col = self._formatter._pformat_col
868         lines, outs = _pformat_col(self, max_lines, show_name=show_name, show_unit=show_unit,
869                                    show_dtype=show_dtype)
870 
871         n_header = outs['n_header']
872         for i, line in enumerate(lines):
873             if i < n_header:
874                 color_print(line, 'red')
875             else:
876                 print(line)
877

904         """

909     @property
910     def unit(self):
911         """
912         The unit associated with this column.  May be a string or a
913         `astropy.units.UnitBase` instance.
914 
915         Setting the ``unit`` property does not change the values of the
916         data.  To perform a unit conversion, use ``convert_unit_to``.
917         """
918         return self._unit
919 
920     @unit.setter
921     def unit(self, unit):
922         if unit is None:
923             self._unit = None
924         else:
925             self._unit = Unit(unit, parse_strict='silent')
926 
927     @unit.deleter
928     def unit(self):
929         self._unit = None
930 
931     def searchsorted(self, v, side='left', sorter=None):
932         # For bytes type data, encode the `v` value as UTF-8 (if necessary) before
933         # calling searchsorted. This prevents a factor of 1000 slowdown in
934         # searchsorted in this case.
935         a = self.data
936         if a.dtype.kind == 'S' and not isinstance(v, bytes):
937             v = np.asarray(v)
938             if v.dtype.kind == 'U':
939                 v = np.char.encode(v, 'utf-8')
940         return np.searchsorted(a, v, side=side, sorter=sorter)
941     searchsorted.__doc__ = np.ndarray.searchsorted.__doc__
942 
943     def convert_unit_to(self, new_unit, equivalencies=[]):
944         """
945         Converts the values of the column in-place from the current
946         unit to the given unit.
947 
948         To change the unit associated with this column without
949         actually changing the data values, simply set the ``unit``
950         property.
951 
952         Parameters
953         ----------
954         new_unit : str or `astropy.units.UnitBase` instance
955             The unit to convert to.
956 
957         equivalencies : list of tuple
958            A list of equivalence pairs to try if the unit are not
959            directly convertible.  See :ref:`astropy:unit_equivalencies`.
960 
961         Raises
962         ------
963         astropy.units.UnitsError
964             If units are inconsistent
965         """
966         if self.unit is None:
967             raise ValueError("No unit set on column")
968         self.data[:] = self.unit.to(
969             new_unit, self.data, equivalencies=equivalencies)
970         self.unit = new_unit
971 
972     @property
973     def groups(self):
974         if not hasattr(self, '_groups'):
975             self._groups = groups.ColumnGroups(self)
976         return self._groups
977 
978     def group_by(self, keys):
979         """
980         Group this column by the specified ``keys``
981 
982         This effectively splits the column into groups which correspond to
983         unique values of the ``keys`` grouping object.  The output is a new
984         `Column` or `MaskedColumn` which contains a copy of this column but
985         sorted by row according to ``keys``.
986 
987         The ``keys`` input to ``group_by`` must be a numpy array with the
988         same length as this column.
989 
990         Parameters
991         ----------
992         keys : numpy array
993             Key grouping object
994 
995         Returns
996         -------
997         out : Column
998             New column with groups attribute set accordingly
999         """
1000         return groups.column_group_by(self, keys)
1001 
1002     def _copy_groups(self, out):
1003         """
1004         Copy current groups into a copy of self ``out``
1005         """
1006         if self.parent_table:
1007             if hasattr(self.parent_table, '_groups'):
1008                 out._groups = groups.ColumnGroups(out, indices=self.parent_table._groups._indices)
1009         elif hasattr(self, '_groups'):
1010             out._groups = groups.ColumnGroups(out, indices=self._groups._indices)
1011 
1012     # Strip off the BaseColumn-ness for repr and str so that
1013     # MaskedColumn.data __repr__ does not include masked_BaseColumn(data =
1014     # [1 2], ...).
1015     def __repr__(self):
1016         return np.asarray(self).__repr__()
1017 
1018     @property
1019     def quantity(self):
1020         """
1021         A view of this table column as a `~astropy.units.Quantity` object with
1022         units given by the Column's `unit` parameter.
1023         """
1024         # the Quantity initializer is used here because it correctly fails
1025         # if the column's values are non-numeric (like strings), while .view
1026         # will happily return a quantity with gibberish for numerical values
1027         return Quantity(self, self.unit, copy=False, dtype=self.dtype, order='A', subok=True)
1028 
1029     def to(self, unit, equivalencies=[], **kwargs):
1030         """
1031         Converts this table column to a `~astropy.units.Quantity` object with
1032         the requested units.
1033 
1034         Parameters
1035         ----------
1036         unit : unit-like
1037             The unit to convert to (i.e., a valid argument to the
1038             :meth:`astropy.units.Quantity.to` method).
1039         equivalencies : list of tuple
1040             Equivalencies to use for this conversion.  See
1041             :meth:`astropy.units.Quantity.to` for more details.
1042 
1043         Returns
1044         -------
1045         quantity : `~astropy.units.Quantity`
1046             A quantity object with the contents of this column in the units
1047             ``unit``.
1048         """
1049         return self.quantity.to(unit, equivalencies)
1050 
1051     def _copy_attrs(self, obj):
1052         """
1053         Copy key column attributes from ``obj`` to self
1054         """
1055         for attr in ('name', 'unit', '_format', 'description'):
1056             val = getattr(obj, attr, None)
1057             setattr(self, attr, val)
1058 
1059         # Light copy of meta if it is not empty
1060         obj_meta = getattr(obj, 'meta', None)
1061         if obj_meta:
1062             self.meta = obj_meta.copy()
1063 
1064     @staticmethod
1065     def _encode_str(value):
1066         """
1067         Encode anything that is unicode-ish as utf-8.  This method is only
1068         called for Py3+.
1069         """
1070         if isinstance(value, str):
1071             value = value.encode('utf-8')
1072         elif isinstance(value, bytes) or value is np.ma.masked:
1073             pass
1074         else:
1075             arr = np.asarray(value)
1076             if arr.dtype.char == 'U':
1077                 arr = np.char.encode(arr, encoding='utf-8')
1078                 if isinstance(value, np.ma.MaskedArray):
1079                     arr = np.ma.array(arr, mask=value.mask, copy=False)
1080             value = arr
1081 
1082         return value
1083 
1084     def tolist(self):
1085         if self.dtype.kind == 'S':
1086             return np.chararray.decode(self, encoding='utf-8').tolist()
1087         else:
1088             return super().tolist()
1089 
1090 
1091 class Column(BaseColumn):
1092     """Define a data column for use in a Table object.
1093 
1094     Parameters
1095     ----------
1096     data : list, ndarray, or None
1097         Column data values
1098     name : str
1099         Column name and key for reference within Table
1100     dtype : `~numpy.dtype`-like
1101         Data type for column
1102     shape : tuple or ()
1103         Dimensions of a single row element in the column data
1104     length : int or 0
1105         Number of row elements in column data
1106     description : str or None
1107         Full description of column
1108     unit : str or None
1109         Physical unit
1110     format : str, None, or callable
1111         Format string for outputting column values.  This can be an
1112         "old-style" (``format % value``) or "new-style" (`str.format`)
1113         format specification string or a function or any callable object that
1114         accepts a single value and returns a string.
1115     meta : dict-like or None
1116         Meta-data associated with the column
1117 
1118     Examples
1119     --------
1120     A Column can be created in two different ways:
1121 
1122     - Provide a ``data`` value but not ``shape`` or ``length`` (which are
1123       inferred from the data).
1124 
1125       Examples::
1126 
1127         col = Column(data=[1, 2], name='name')  # shape=(2,)
1128         col = Column(data=[[1, 2], [3, 4]], name='name')  # shape=(2, 2)
1129         col = Column(data=[1, 2], name='name', dtype=float)
1130         col = Column(data=np.array([1, 2]), name='name')
1131         col = Column(data=['hello', 'world'], name='name')
1132 
1133       The ``dtype`` argument can be any value which is an acceptable
1134       fixed-size data-type initializer for the numpy.dtype() method.  See
1135       `<https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.dtypes.html>`_.
1136       Examples include:
1137 
1138       - Python non-string type (float, int, bool)
1139       - Numpy non-string type (e.g. np.float32, np.int64, np.bool\\_)
1140       - Numpy.dtype array-protocol type strings (e.g. 'i4', 'f8', 'S15')
1141 
1142       If no ``dtype`` value is provide then the type is inferred using
1143       ``np.array(data)``.
1144 
1145     - Provide ``length`` and optionally ``shape``, but not ``data``
1146 
1147       Examples::
1148 
1149         col = Column(name='name', length=5)
1150         col = Column(name='name', dtype=int, length=10, shape=(3,4))
1151 
1152       The default ``dtype`` is ``np.float64``.  The ``shape`` argument is the
1153       array shape of a single cell in the column.
1154     """
1155 
1156     def __new__(cls, data=None, name=None,
1157                 dtype=None, shape=(), length=0,
1158                 description=None, unit=None, format=None, meta=None,
1159                 copy=False, copy_indices=True):
1160 
1161         if isinstance(data, MaskedColumn) and np.any(data.mask):
1162             raise TypeError("Cannot convert a MaskedColumn with masked value to a Column")
1163 
1164         self = super().__new__(
1165             cls, data=data, name=name, dtype=dtype, shape=shape, length=length,
1166             description=description, unit=unit, format=format, meta=meta,
1167             copy=copy, copy_indices=copy_indices)

1170     def __setattr__(self, item, value):

1172             raise AttributeError("cannot set mask value to a column in non-masked Table")

1175         if item == 'unit' and issubclass(self.dtype.type, np.number):
1176             try:
1177                 converted = self.parent_table._convert_col_for_table(self)
1178             except AttributeError:  # Either no parent table or parent table is None
1179                 pass
1180             else:
1181                 if converted is not self:
1182                     self.parent_table.replace_column(self.name, converted)

1184     def _base_repr_(self, html=False):
1185         # If scalar then just convert to correct numpy type and use numpy repr
1186         if self.ndim == 0:
1187             return repr(self.item())
1188 
1189         descr_vals = [self.__class__.__name__]
1190         unit = None if self.unit is None else str(self.unit)
1191         shape = None if self.ndim <= 1 else self.shape[1:]
1192         for attr, val in (('name', self.name),
1193                           ('dtype', dtype_info_name(self.dtype)),
1194                           ('shape', shape),
1195                           ('unit', unit),
1196                           ('format', self.format),
1197                           ('description', self.description),
1198                           ('length', len(self))):
1199 
1200             if val is not None:
1201                 descr_vals.append(f'{attr}={val!r}')
1202 
1203         descr = '<' + ' '.join(descr_vals) + '>\n'
1204 
1205         if html:
1206             from astropy.utils.xml.writer import xml_escape
1207             descr = xml_escape(descr)
1208 
1209         data_lines, outs = self._formatter._pformat_col(
1210             self, show_name=False, show_unit=False, show_length=False, html=html)
1211 
1212         out = descr + '\n'.join(data_lines)
1213 
1214         return out
