1233     def _check_string_truncate(self, value):
1234         """
1235         Emit a warning if any elements of ``value`` will be truncated when
1236         ``value`` is assigned to self.
1237         """
1238         # Convert input ``value`` to the string dtype of this column and
1239         # find the length of the longest string in the array.
1240         value = np.asanyarray(value, dtype=self.dtype.type)
1241         if value.size == 0:
1242             return
1243         value_str_len = np.char.str_len(value).max()
1244 
1245         # Parse the array-protocol typestring (e.g. '|U15') of self.dtype which
1246         # has the character repeat count on the right side.
1247         self_str_len = dtype_bytes_or_chars(self.dtype)
1248 
1249         if value_str_len > self_str_len:
1250             warnings.warn('truncated right side string(s) longer than {} '
1251                           'character(s) during assignment'
1252                           .format(self_str_len),
1253                           StringTruncateWarning,
1254                           stacklevel=3)
1255 
1256     def __setitem__(self, index, value):
1257         if self.dtype.char == 'S':
1258             value = self._encode_str(value)
1259 
1260         # Issue warning for string assignment that truncates ``value``
1261         if issubclass(self.dtype.type, np.character):
1262             self._check_string_truncate(value)
1263 
1264         # update indices
1265         self.info.adjust_indices(index, value, len(self))
1266 
1267         # Set items using a view of the underlying data, as it gives an
1268         # order-of-magnitude speed-up. [#2994]
1269         self.data[index] = value

1278     def insert(self, obj, values, axis=0):
1279         """
1280         Insert values before the given indices in the column and return
1281         a new `~astropy.table.Column` object.
1282 
1283         Parameters
1284         ----------
1285         obj : int, slice or sequence of int
1286             Object that defines the index or indices before which ``values`` is
1287             inserted.
1288         values : array-like
1289             Value(s) to insert.  If the type of ``values`` is different from
1290             that of the column, ``values`` is converted to the matching type.
1291             ``values`` should be shaped so that it can be broadcast appropriately.
1292         axis : int, optional
1293             Axis along which to insert ``values``.  If ``axis`` is None then
1294             the column array is flattened before insertion.  Default is 0,
1295             which will insert a row.
1296 
1297         Returns
1298         -------
1299         out : `~astropy.table.Column`
1300             A copy of column with ``values`` and ``mask`` inserted.  Note that the
1301             insertion does not occur in-place: a new column is returned.
1302         """
1303         if self.dtype.kind == 'O':
1304             # Even if values is array-like (e.g. [1,2,3]), insert as a single
1305             # object.  Numpy.insert instead inserts each element in an array-like
1306             # input individually.
1307             data = np.insert(self, obj, None, axis=axis)
1308             data[obj] = values
1309         else:
1310             self_for_insert = _expand_string_array_for_values(self, values)
1311             data = np.insert(self_for_insert, obj, values, axis=axis)
1312 
1313         out = data.view(self.__class__)
1314         out.__array_finalize__(self)
1315         return out

1329 class MaskedColumnInfo(ColumnInfo):
1330     """
1331     Container for meta information like name, description, format.
1332 
1333     This is required when the object is used as a mixin column within a table,
1334     but can be used as a general way to store meta information.  In this case
1335     it just adds the ``mask_val`` attribute.
1336     """
1337     # Add `serialize_method` attribute to the attrs that MaskedColumnInfo knows
1338     # about.  This allows customization of the way that MaskedColumn objects
1339     # get written to file depending on format.  The default is to use whatever
1340     # the writer would normally do, which in the case of FITS or ECSV is to use
1341     # a NULL value within the data itself.  If serialize_method is 'data_mask'
1342     # then the mask is explicitly written out as a separate column if there
1343     # are any masked values.  See also code below.
1344     attr_names = ColumnInfo.attr_names | {'serialize_method'}
1345 
1346     # When `serialize_method` is 'data_mask', and data and mask are being written
1347     # as separate columns, use column names <name> and <name>.mask (instead
1348     # of default encoding as <name>.data and <name>.mask).
1349     _represent_as_dict_primary_data = 'data'
1350 
1351     mask_val = np.ma.masked
1352 
1353     def __init__(self, bound=False):
1354         super().__init__(bound)
1355 
1356         # If bound to a data object instance then create the dict of attributes
1357         # which stores the info attribute values.
1358         if bound:
1359             # Specify how to serialize this object depending on context.
1360             self.serialize_method = {'fits': 'null_value',
1361                                      'ecsv': 'null_value',
1362                                      'hdf5': 'data_mask',
1363                                      'parquet': 'data_mask',
1364                                      None: 'null_value'}
1365 
1366     def _represent_as_dict(self):
1367         out = super()._represent_as_dict()
1368         # If we are a structured masked column, then our parent class,
1369         # ColumnInfo, will already have set up a dict with masked parts,
1370         # which will be serialized later, so no further work needed here.
1371         if self._parent.dtype.names is not None:
1372             return out
1373 
1374         col = self._parent
1375 
1376         # If the serialize method for this context (e.g. 'fits' or 'ecsv') is
1377         # 'data_mask', that means to serialize using an explicit mask column.
1378         method = self.serialize_method[self._serialize_context]
1379 
1380         if method == 'data_mask':
1381             # Note: a driver here is a performance issue in #8443 where repr() of a
1382             # np.ma.MaskedArray value is up to 10 times slower than repr of a normal array
1383             # value.  So regardless of whether there are masked elements it is useful to
1384             # explicitly define this as a serialized column and use col.data.data (ndarray)
1385             # instead of letting it fall through to the "standard" serialization machinery.
1386             out['data'] = col.data.data
1387 
1388             if np.any(col.mask):
1389                 # Only if there are actually masked elements do we add the ``mask`` column
1390                 out['mask'] = col.mask
1391 
1392         elif method == 'null_value':
1393             pass
1394 
1395         else:
1396             raise ValueError('serialize method must be either "data_mask" or "null_value"')
1397 
1398         return out
1399

1401 class MaskedColumn(Column, _MaskedColumnGetitemShim, ma.MaskedArray):
1402     """Define a masked data column for use in a Table object.
1403 
1404     Parameters
1405     ----------
1406     data : list, ndarray, or None
1407         Column data values
1408     name : str
1409         Column name and key for reference within Table
1410     mask : list, ndarray or None
1411         Boolean mask for which True indicates missing or invalid data
1412     fill_value : float, int, str, or None
1413         Value used when filling masked column elements
1414     dtype : `~numpy.dtype`-like
1415         Data type for column
1416     shape : tuple or ()
1417         Dimensions of a single row element in the column data
1418     length : int or 0
1419         Number of row elements in column data
1420     description : str or None
1421         Full description of column
1422     unit : str or None
1423         Physical unit
1424     format : str, None, or callable
1425         Format string for outputting column values.  This can be an
1426         "old-style" (``format % value``) or "new-style" (`str.format`)
1427         format specification string or a function or any callable object that
1428         accepts a single value and returns a string.
1429     meta : dict-like or None
1430         Meta-data associated with the column
1431 
1432     Examples
1433     --------
1434     A MaskedColumn is similar to a Column except that it includes ``mask`` and
1435     ``fill_value`` attributes.  It can be created in two different ways:
1436 
1437     - Provide a ``data`` value but not ``shape`` or ``length`` (which are
1438       inferred from the data).
1439 
1440       Examples::
1441 
1442         col = MaskedColumn(data=[1, 2], name='name')
1443         col = MaskedColumn(data=[1, 2], name='name', mask=[True, False])
1444         col = MaskedColumn(data=[1, 2], name='name', dtype=float, fill_value=99)
1445 
1446       The ``mask`` argument will be cast as a boolean array and specifies
1447       which elements are considered to be missing or invalid.
1448 
1449       The ``dtype`` argument can be any value which is an acceptable
1450       fixed-size data-type initializer for the numpy.dtype() method.  See
1451       `<https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.dtypes.html>`_.
1452       Examples include:
1453 
1454       - Python non-string type (float, int, bool)
1455       - Numpy non-string type (e.g. np.float32, np.int64, np.bool\\_)
1456       - Numpy.dtype array-protocol type strings (e.g. 'i4', 'f8', 'S15')
1457 
1458       If no ``dtype`` value is provide then the type is inferred using
1459       ``np.array(data)``.  When ``data`` is provided then the ``shape``
1460       and ``length`` arguments are ignored.
1461 
1462     - Provide ``length`` and optionally ``shape``, but not ``data``
1463 
1464       Examples::
1465 
1466         col = MaskedColumn(name='name', length=5)
1467         col = MaskedColumn(name='name', dtype=int, length=10, shape=(3,4))
1468 
1469       The default ``dtype`` is ``np.float64``.  The ``shape`` argument is the
1470       array shape of a single cell in the column.
1471     """
1472     info = MaskedColumnInfo()
1473 
1474     def __new__(cls, data=None, name=None, mask=None, fill_value=None,
1475                 dtype=None, shape=(), length=0,
1476                 description=None, unit=None, format=None, meta=None,
1477                 copy=False, copy_indices=True):
1478 
1479         if mask is None:
1480             # If mask is None then we need to determine the mask (if any) from the data.
1481             # The naive method is looking for a mask attribute on data, but this can fail,
1482             # see #8816.  Instead use ``MaskedArray`` to do the work.
1483             mask = ma.MaskedArray(data).mask
1484             if mask is np.ma.nomask:
1485                 # Handle odd-ball issue with np.ma.nomask (numpy #13758), and see below.
1486                 mask = False
1487             elif copy:
1488                 mask = mask.copy()
1489 
1490         elif mask is np.ma.nomask:
1491             # Force the creation of a full mask array as nomask is tricky to
1492             # use and will fail in an unexpected manner when setting a value
1493             # to the mask.
1494             mask = False
1495         else:
1496             mask = deepcopy(mask)
1497 
1498         # Create self using MaskedArray as a wrapper class, following the example of
1499         # class MSubArray in
1500         # https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/maintenance/1.8.x/numpy/ma/tests/test_subclassing.py
1501         # This pattern makes it so that __array_finalize__ is called as expected (e.g. #1471 and
1502         # https://github.com/astropy/astropy/commit/ff6039e8)
1503 
1504         # First just pass through all args and kwargs to BaseColumn, then wrap that object
1505         # with MaskedArray.
1506         self_data = BaseColumn(data, dtype=dtype, shape=shape, length=length, name=name,
1507                                unit=unit, format=format, description=description,
1508                                meta=meta, copy=copy, copy_indices=copy_indices)
1509         self = ma.MaskedArray.__new__(cls, data=self_data, mask=mask)
1510         # The above process preserves info relevant for Column, but this does
1511         # not include serialize_method (and possibly other future attributes)
1512         # relevant for MaskedColumn, so we set info explicitly.
1513         if 'info' in getattr(data, '__dict__', {}):
1514             self.info = data.info
1515 
1516         # Note: do not set fill_value in the MaskedArray constructor because this does not
1517         # go through the fill_value workarounds.
1518         if fill_value is None and getattr(data, 'fill_value', None) is not None:
1519             # Coerce the fill_value to the correct type since `data` may be a
1520             # different dtype than self.
1521             fill_value = np.array(data.fill_value, self.dtype)[()]
1522         self.fill_value = fill_value
1523 
1524         self.parent_table = None
1525 
1526         # needs to be done here since self doesn't come from BaseColumn.__new__
1527         for index in self.indices:
1528             index.replace_col(self_data, self)
1529 
1530         return self
1531 
1532     @property
1533     def fill_value(self):
1534         return self.get_fill_value()  # defer to native ma.MaskedArray method
1535 
1536     @fill_value.setter
1537     def fill_value(self, val):
1538         """Set fill value both in the masked column view and in the parent table
1539         if it exists.  Setting one or the other alone doesn't work."""
1540 
1541         # another ma bug workaround: If the value of fill_value for a string array is
1542         # requested but not yet set then it gets created as 'N/A'.  From this point onward
1543         # any new fill_values are truncated to 3 characters.  Note that this does not
1544         # occur if the masked array is a structured array (as in the previous block that
1545         # deals with the parent table).
1546         #
1547         # >>> x = ma.array(['xxxx'])
1548         # >>> x.fill_value  # fill_value now gets represented as an 'S3' array
1549         # 'N/A'
1550         # >>> x.fill_value='yyyy'
1551         # >>> x.fill_value
1552         # 'yyy'
1553         #
1554         # To handle this we are forced to reset a private variable first:
1555         self._fill_value = None
1556 
1557         self.set_fill_value(val)  # defer to native ma.MaskedArray method
1558 
1559     @property
1560     def data(self):
1561         """The plain MaskedArray data held by this column."""
1562         out = self.view(np.ma.MaskedArray)
1563         # By default, a MaskedArray view will set the _baseclass to be the
1564         # same as that of our own class, i.e., BaseColumn.  Since we want
1565         # to return a plain MaskedArray, we reset the baseclass accordingly.
1566         out._baseclass = np.ndarray
1567         return out
1568 
1569     def filled(self, fill_value=None):
1570         """Return a copy of self, with masked values filled with a given value.
1571 
1572         Parameters
1573         ----------
1574         fill_value : scalar; optional
1575             The value to use for invalid entries (`None` by default).  If
1576             `None`, the ``fill_value`` attribute of the array is used
1577             instead.
1578 
1579         Returns
1580         -------
1581         filled_column : Column
1582             A copy of ``self`` with masked entries replaced by `fill_value`
1583             (be it the function argument or the attribute of ``self``).
1584         """
1585         if fill_value is None:
1586             fill_value = self.fill_value
1587 
1588         data = super().filled(fill_value)
1589         # Use parent table definition of Column if available
1590         column_cls = self.parent_table.Column if (self.parent_table is not None) else Column
1591 
1592         out = column_cls(name=self.name, data=data, unit=self.unit,
1593                          format=self.format, description=self.description,
1594                          meta=deepcopy(self.meta))
1595         return out
1596 
1597     def insert(self, obj, values, mask=None, axis=0):
1598         """
1599         Insert values along the given axis before the given indices and return
1600         a new `~astropy.table.MaskedColumn` object.
1601 
1602         Parameters
1603         ----------
1604         obj : int, slice or sequence of int
1605             Object that defines the index or indices before which ``values`` is
1606             inserted.
1607         values : array-like
1608             Value(s) to insert.  If the type of ``values`` is different from
1609             that of the column, ``values`` is converted to the matching type.
1610             ``values`` should be shaped so that it can be broadcast appropriately.
1611         mask : bool or array-like
1612             Mask value(s) to insert.  If not supplied, and values does not have
1613             a mask either, then False is used.
1614         axis : int, optional
1615             Axis along which to insert ``values``.  If ``axis`` is None then
1616             the column array is flattened before insertion.  Default is 0,
1617             which will insert a row.
1618 
1619         Returns
1620         -------
1621         out : `~astropy.table.MaskedColumn`
1622             A copy of column with ``values`` and ``mask`` inserted.  Note that the
1623             insertion does not occur in-place: a new masked column is returned.
1624         """
1625         self_ma = self.data  # self viewed as MaskedArray
1626 
1627         if self.dtype.kind == 'O':
1628             # Even if values is array-like (e.g. [1,2,3]), insert as a single
1629             # object.  Numpy.insert instead inserts each element in an array-like
1630             # input individually.
1631             new_data = np.insert(self_ma.data, obj, None, axis=axis)
1632             new_data[obj] = values
1633         else:
1634             self_ma = _expand_string_array_for_values(self_ma, values)
1635             new_data = np.insert(self_ma.data, obj, values, axis=axis)
1636 
1637         if mask is None:
1638             mask = getattr(values, 'mask', np.ma.nomask)
1639             if mask is np.ma.nomask:
1640                 if self.dtype.kind == 'O':
1641                     mask = False
1642                 else:
1643                     mask = np.zeros(np.shape(values), dtype=bool)
1644 
1645         new_mask = np.insert(self_ma.mask, obj, mask, axis=axis)
1646         new_ma = np.ma.array(new_data, mask=new_mask, copy=False)
1647 
1648         out = new_ma.view(self.__class__)
1649         out.parent_table = None
1650         out.indices = []
1651         out._copy_attrs(self)
1652         out.fill_value = self.fill_value
1653 
1654         return out
1655 
1656     def _copy_attrs_slice(self, out):
1657         # Fixes issue #3023: when calling getitem with a MaskedArray subclass
1658         # the original object attributes are not copied.
1659         if out.__class__ is self.__class__:
1660             # TODO: this part is essentially the same as what is done in
1661             # __array_finalize__ and could probably be called directly in our
1662             # override of __getitem__ in _columns_mixins.pyx). Refactor?
1663             if 'info' in self.__dict__:
1664                 out.info = self.info
1665             out.parent_table = None
1666             # we need this because __getitem__ does a shallow copy of indices
1667             if out.indices is self.indices:
1668                 out.indices = []
1669             out._copy_attrs(self)
1670         return out
1671 
1672     def __setitem__(self, index, value):
1673         # Issue warning for string assignment that truncates ``value``
1674         if self.dtype.char == 'S':
1675             value = self._encode_str(value)
1676 
1677         if issubclass(self.dtype.type, np.character):
1678             # Account for a bug in np.ma.MaskedArray setitem.
1679             # https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/8624
1680             value = np.ma.asanyarray(value, dtype=self.dtype.type)
1681 
1682             # Check for string truncation after filling masked items with
1683             # empty (zero-length) string.  Note that filled() does not make
1684             # a copy if there are no masked items.
1685             self._check_string_truncate(value.filled(''))
1686 
1687         # update indices
1688         self.info.adjust_indices(index, value, len(self))
1689 
1690         ma.MaskedArray.__setitem__(self, index, value)

369 def resolve_astropy_and_dev_reference(app, env, node, contnode):
370     """
371     Reference targets for ``astropy:`` and ``astropy-dev:`` are special cases.
372 
373     Documentation links in astropy can be set up as intersphinx links so that
374     affiliate packages do not have to override the docstrings when building
375     the docs.
376 
377     If we are building the development docs it is a local ref targeting the
378     label ``astropy-dev:<label>``, but for stable docs it should be an
379     intersphinx resolution to the development docs.
380 
381     See https://github.com/astropy/astropy/issues/11366
382     """
383     # should the node be processed?
384     reftarget = node.get('reftarget')  # str or None
385     if str(reftarget).startswith('astropy:'):
386         # This allows Astropy to use intersphinx links to itself and have
387         # them resolve to local links. Downstream packages will see intersphinx.
388         # TODO! deprecate this if sphinx-doc/sphinx/issues/9169 is implemented.
389         process, replace = True, 'astropy:'
390     elif dev and str(reftarget).startswith('astropy-dev:'):
391         process, replace = True, 'astropy-dev:'
392     else:
393         process, replace = False, ''
394 
395     # make link local
396     if process:
397         reftype = node.get('reftype')
398         refdoc = node.get('refdoc', app.env.docname)
399         # convert astropy intersphinx targets to local links.
400         # there are a few types of intersphinx link patters, as described in
401         # https://docs.readthedocs.io/en/stable/guides/intersphinx.html
402         reftarget = reftarget.replace(replace, '')
403         if reftype == "doc":  # also need to replace the doc link
404             node.replace_attr("reftarget", reftarget)
405         # Delegate to the ref node's original domain/target (typically :ref:)
406         try:
407             domain = app.env.domains[node['refdomain']]
408             return domain.resolve_xref(app.env, refdoc, app.builder,
409                                        reftype, reftarget, node, contnode)
410         except Exception:
411             pass
412 
413         # Otherwise return None which should delegate to intersphinx
414 
415

417     if sphinx_gallery is None:
418         msg = ('The sphinx_gallery extension is not installed, so the '
419                'gallery will not be built.  You will probably see '
420                'additional warnings about undefined references due '
421                'to this.')
422         try:
423             app.warn(msg)
424         except AttributeError:

<issue>
url_for can't distinguish a blueprint mounted two times
Based on blueprint concept, I expected it to handle relative `url_for` nicely:

```
from flask import Blueprint, Flask, url_for

bp = Blueprint('foo', __name__)

@bp.route('/')
def func():
    return url_for('.func')

app = Flask(__name__)
app.register_blueprint(bp, url_prefix='/foo')
app.register_blueprint(bp, url_prefix='/bar')

client = app.test_client()
print client.get('/foo/').data
print client.get('/bar/').data
```

Both prints write the URL to the first blueprint registered (`/foo/`). Is it possible to mount two times the same blueprint and make relative `url_for` work? Is this behaviour expected?


</issue>

36     user_id = session.get("user_id")
37 
38     if user_id is None:
39         g.user = None
40     else:
41         g.user = (
42             get_db().execute("SELECT * FROM user WHERE id = ?", (user_id,)).fetchone()
43         )
44 
45 
46 @bp.route("/register", methods=("GET", "POST"))
47 def register():
48     """Register a new user.
49 
50     Validates that the username is not already taken. Hashes the
51     password for security.
52     """
53     if request.method == "POST":
54         username = request.form["username"]
55         password = request.form["password"]
56         db = get_db()
57         error = None
58 
59         if not username:
60             error = "Username is required."
61         elif not password:
62             error = "Password is required."
63         elif (
64             db.execute("SELECT id FROM user WHERE username = ?", (username,)).fetchone()
65             is not None
66         ):
67             error = f"User {username} is already registered."
68 
69         if error is None:
70             # the name is available, store it in the database and go to
71             # the login page
72             db.execute(
73                 "INSERT INTO user (username, password) VALUES (?, ?)",
74                 (username, generate_password_hash(password)),
75             )
76             db.commit()
77             return redirect(url_for("auth.login"))
78 
79         flash(error)
80 
81     return render_template("auth/register.html")
82 
83 
84 @bp.route("/login", methods=("GET", "POST"))
85 def login():
86     """Log in a registered user by adding the user id to the session."""
87     if request.method == "POST":
88         username = request.form["username"]
89         password = request.form["password"]
90         db = get_db()
91         error = None
92         user = db.execute(
93             "SELECT * FROM user WHERE username = ?", (username,)
94         ).fetchone()
95 
96         if user is None:
97             error = "Incorrect username."
98         elif not check_password_hash(user["password"], password):
99             error = "Incorrect password."
100 
101         if error is None:
102             # store the user id in a new session and return to the index
103             session.clear()
104             session["user_id"] = user["id"]
105             return redirect(url_for("index"))
106 
107         flash(error)
108 
109     return render_template("auth/login.html")
110 
111 
112 @bp.route("/logout")
113 def logout():
114     """Clear the current session, including the stored user id."""
115     session.clear()
116     return redirect(url_for("index"))
117

13 bp = Blueprint("blog", __name__)

16 @bp.route("/")
17 def index():
18     """Show all the posts, most recent first."""
19     db = get_db()
20     posts = db.execute(
21         "SELECT p.id, title, body, created, author_id, username"
22         " FROM post p JOIN user u ON p.author_id = u.id"
23         " ORDER BY created DESC"
24     ).fetchall()
25     return render_template("blog/index.html", posts=posts)
26 
27 
28 def get_post(id, check_author=True):
29     """Get a post and its author by id.
30 
31     Checks that the id exists and optionally that the current user is
32     the author.
33 
34     :param id: id of post to get
35     :param check_author: require the current user to be the author
36     :return: the post with author information
37     :raise 404: if a post with the given id doesn't exist
38     :raise 403: if the current user isn't the author
39     """
40     post = (
41         get_db()
42         .execute(
43             "SELECT p.id, title, body, created, author_id, username"
44             " FROM post p JOIN user u ON p.author_id = u.id"
45             " WHERE p.id = ?",
46             (id,),
47         )
48         .fetchone()
49     )
50 
51     if post is None:
52         abort(404, f"Post id {id} doesn't exist.")
53 
54     if check_author and post["author_id"] != g.user["id"]:
55         abort(403)
56 
57     return post
58 
59 
60 @bp.route("/create", methods=("GET", "POST"))
61 @login_required
62 def create():
63     """Create a new post for the current user."""
64     if request.method == "POST":
65         title = request.form["title"]
66         body = request.form["body"]
67         error = None
68 
69         if not title:
70             error = "Title is required."
71 
72         if error is not None:
73             flash(error)
74         else:
75             db = get_db()
76             db.execute(
77                 "INSERT INTO post (title, body, author_id) VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
78                 (title, body, g.user["id"]),
79             )
80             db.commit()
81             return redirect(url_for("blog.index"))
82 
83     return render_template("blog/create.html")
84 
85 
86 @bp.route("/<int:id>/update", methods=("GET", "POST"))
87 @login_required
88 def update(id):
89     """Update a post if the current user is the author."""
90     post = get_post(id)
91 
92     if request.method == "POST":
93         title = request.form["title"]
94         body = request.form["body"]
95         error = None
96 
97         if not title:
98             error = "Title is required."
99 
100         if error is not None:
101             flash(error)
102         else:
103             db = get_db()
104             db.execute(
105                 "UPDATE post SET title = ?, body = ? WHERE id = ?", (title, body, id)
106             )
107             db.commit()
108             return redirect(url_for("blog.index"))
109 
110     return render_template("blog/update.html", post=post)
111 
112 
113 @bp.route("/<int:id>/delete", methods=("POST",))
114 @login_required
115 def delete(id):
116     """Delete a post.
117 
118     Ensures that the post exists and that the logged in user is the
119     author of the post.
120     """
121     get_post(id)
122     db = get_db()
123     db.execute("DELETE FROM post WHERE id = ?", (id,))
124     db.commit()
125     return redirect(url_for("blog.index"))

84     def iscoroutinefunction(func: t.Any) -> bool:
85         while inspect.ismethod(func):
86             func = func.__func__
87 
88         while isinstance(func, functools.partial):
89             func = func.func
90 
91         return inspect.iscoroutinefunction(func)

95     if value is None or isinstance(value, timedelta):

101 class Flask(Scaffold):
102     """The flask object implements a WSGI application and acts as the central
103     object.  It is passed the name of the module or package of the
104     application.  Once it is created it will act as a central registry for
105     the view functions, the URL rules, template configuration and much more.
106 
107     The name of the package is used to resolve resources from inside the
108     package or the folder the module is contained in depending on if the
109     package parameter resolves to an actual python package (a folder with
110     an :file:`__init__.py` file inside) or a standard module (just a ``.py`` file).
111 
112     For more information about resource loading, see :func:`open_resource`.
113 
114     Usually you create a :class:`Flask` instance in your main module or
115     in the :file:`__init__.py` file of your package like this::
116 
117         from flask import Flask
118         app = Flask(__name__)
119 
120     .. admonition:: About the First Parameter
121 
122         The idea of the first parameter is to give Flask an idea of what
123         belongs to your application.  This name is used to find resources
124         on the filesystem, can be used by extensions to improve debugging
125         information and a lot more.
126 
127         So it's important what you provide there.  If you are using a single
128         module, `__name__` is always the correct value.  If you however are
129         using a package, it's usually recommended to hardcode the name of
130         your package there.
131 
132         For example if your application is defined in :file:`yourapplication/app.py`
133         you should create it with one of the two versions below::
134 
135             app = Flask('yourapplication')
136             app = Flask(__name__.split('.')[0])
137 
138         Why is that?  The application will work even with `__name__`, thanks
139         to how resources are looked up.  However it will make debugging more
140         painful.  Certain extensions can make assumptions based on the
141         import name of your application.  For example the Flask-SQLAlchemy
142         extension will look for the code in your application that triggered
143         an SQL query in debug mode.  If the import name is not properly set
144         up, that debugging information is lost.  (For example it would only
145         pick up SQL queries in `yourapplication.app` and not
146         `yourapplication.views.frontend`)
147 
148     .. versionadded:: 0.7
149        The `static_url_path`, `static_folder`, and `template_folder`
150        parameters were added.
151 
152     .. versionadded:: 0.8
153        The `instance_path` and `instance_relative_config` parameters were
154        added.
155 
156     .. versionadded:: 0.11
157        The `root_path` parameter was added.
158 
159     .. versionadded:: 1.0
160        The ``host_matching`` and ``static_host`` parameters were added.
161 
162     .. versionadded:: 1.0
163        The ``subdomain_matching`` parameter was added. Subdomain
164        matching needs to be enabled manually now. Setting
165        :data:`SERVER_NAME` does not implicitly enable it.
166 
167     :param import_name: the name of the application package
168     :param static_url_path: can be used to specify a different path for the
169                             static files on the web.  Defaults to the name
170                             of the `static_folder` folder.
171     :param static_folder: The folder with static files that is served at
172         ``static_url_path``. Relative to the application ``root_path``
173         or an absolute path. Defaults to ``'static'``.
174     :param static_host: the host to use when adding the static route.
175         Defaults to None. Required when using ``host_matching=True``
176         with a ``static_folder`` configured.
177     :param host_matching: set ``url_map.host_matching`` attribute.
178         Defaults to False.
179     :param subdomain_matching: consider the subdomain relative to
180         :data:`SERVER_NAME` when matching routes. Defaults to False.
181     :param template_folder: the folder that contains the templates that should
182                             be used by the application.  Defaults to
183                             ``'templates'`` folder in the root path of the
184                             application.
185     :param instance_path: An alternative instance path for the application.
186                           By default the folder ``'instance'`` next to the
187                           package or module is assumed to be the instance
188                           path.
189     :param instance_relative_config: if set to ``True`` relative filenames
190                                      for loading the config are assumed to
191                                      be relative to the instance path instead
192                                      of the application root.
193     :param root_path: The path to the root of the application files.
194         This should only be set manually when it can't be detected
195         automatically, such as for namespace packages.
196     """
197 
198     #: The class that is used for request objects.  See :class:`~flask.Request`
199     #: for more information.
200     request_class = Request
201 
202     #: The class that is used for response objects.  See
203     #: :class:`~flask.Response` for more information.
204     response_class = Response
205

207     #:

213     #: Example use cases for a custom class:

386     def __init__(
387         self,
388         import_name: str,
389         static_url_path: t.Optional[str] = None,
390         static_folder: t.Optional[str] = "static",
391         static_host: t.Optional[str] = None,

395         instance_path: t.Optional[str] = None,
396         instance_relative_config: bool = False,
397         root_path: t.Optional[str] = None,
398     ):
399         super().__init__(
400             import_name=import_name,
401             static_folder=static_folder,
402             static_url_path=static_url_path,
403             template_folder=template_folder,
404             root_path=root_path,
405         )
406 
407         if instance_path is None:
408             instance_path = self.auto_find_instance_path()
409         elif not os.path.isabs(instance_path):
410             raise ValueError(
411                 "If an instance path is provided it must be absolute."
412                 " A relative path was given instead."
413             )

415         #: Holds the path to the instance folder.
416         #:
417         #: .. versionadded:: 0.8
418         self.instance_path = instance_path
419 
420         #: The configuration dictionary as :class:`Config`.  This behaves
421         #: exactly like a regular dictionary but supports additional methods
422         #: to load a config from files.
423         self.config = self.make_config(instance_relative_config)
424 
425         #: A list of functions that are called when :meth:`url_for` raises a
426         #: :exc:`~werkzeug.routing.BuildError`.  Each function registered here
427         #: is called with `error`, `endpoint` and `values`.  If a function
428         #: returns ``None`` or raises a :exc:`BuildError` the next function is
429         #: tried.
430         #:
431         #: .. versionadded:: 0.9
432         self.url_build_error_handlers: t.List[
433             t.Callable[[Exception, str, dict], str]
434         ] = []
435 
436         #: A list of functions that will be called at the beginning of the
437         #: first request to this instance. To register a function, use the
438         #: :meth:`before_first_request` decorator.
439         #:
440         #: .. versionadded:: 0.8
441         self.before_first_request_funcs: t.List[BeforeRequestCallable] = []
442 
443         #: A list of functions that are called when the application context
444         #: is destroyed.  Since the application context is also torn down
445         #: if the request ends this is the place to store code that disconnects
446         #: from databases.
447         #:
448         #: .. versionadded:: 0.9
449         self.teardown_appcontext_funcs: t.List[TeardownCallable] = []
450 
451         #: A list of shell context processor functions that should be run
452         #: when a shell context is created.
453         #:
454         #: .. versionadded:: 0.11
455         self.shell_context_processors: t.List[t.Callable[[], t.Dict[str, t.Any]]] = []
456 
457         #: Maps registered blueprint names to blueprint objects. The
458         #: dict retains the order the blueprints were registered in.
459         #: Blueprints can be registered multiple times, this dict does
460         #: not track how often they were attached.
461         #:
462         #: .. versionadded:: 0.7
463         self.blueprints: t.Dict[str, "Blueprint"] = {}
464 
465         #: a place where extensions can store application specific state.  For
466         #: example this is where an extension could store database engines and
467         #: similar things.
468         #:
469         #: The key must match the name of the extension module. For example in
470         #: case of a "Flask-Foo" extension in `flask_foo`, the key would be
471         #: ``'foo'``.
472         #:
473         #: .. versionadded:: 0.7
474         self.extensions: dict = {}
475 
476         #: The :class:`~werkzeug.routing.Map` for this instance.  You can use
477         #: this to change the routing converters after the class was created
478         #: but before any routes are connected.  Example::
479         #:
480         #:    from werkzeug.routing import BaseConverter
481         #:
482         #:    class ListConverter(BaseConverter):
483         #:        def to_python(self, value):
484         #:            return value.split(',')
485         #:        def to_url(self, values):
486         #:            return ','.join(super(ListConverter, self).to_url(value)
487         #:                            for value in values)
488         #:
489         #:    app = Flask(__name__)
490         #:    app.url_map.converters['list'] = ListConverter
491         self.url_map = self.url_map_class()
492 
493         self.url_map.host_matching = host_matching
494         self.subdomain_matching = subdomain_matching
495 
496         # tracks internally if the application already handled at least one
497         # request.
498         self._got_first_request = False
499         self._before_request_lock = Lock()
500 
501         # Add a static route using the provided static_url_path, static_host,
502         # and static_folder if there is a configured static_folder.
503         # Note we do this without checking if static_folder exists.
504         # For one, it might be created while the server is running (e.g. during
505         # development). Also, Google App Engine stores static files somewhere
506         if self.has_static_folder:
507             assert (
508                 bool(static_host) == host_matching
509             ), "Invalid static_host/host_matching combination"
510             # Use a weakref to avoid creating a reference cycle between the app
511             # and the view function (see #3761).
512             self_ref = weakref.ref(self)
513             self.add_url_rule(
514                 f"{self.static_url_path}/<path:filename>",
515                 endpoint="static",
516                 host=static_host,
517                 view_func=lambda **kw: self_ref().send_static_file(**kw),  # type: ignore # noqa: B950
518             )
519 
520         # Set the name of the Click group in case someone wants to add
521         # the app's commands to another CLI tool.
522         self.cli.name = self.name
523 
524     def _is_setup_finished(self) -> bool:
525         return self.debug and self._got_first_request

527     @locked_cached_property
528     def name(self) -> str:  # type: ignore
529         """The name of the application.  This is usually the import name
530         with the difference that it's guessed from the run file if the
531         import name is main.  This name is used as a display name when
532         Flask needs the name of the application.  It can be set and overridden
533         to change the value.
534 
535         .. versionadded:: 0.8
536         """
537         if self.import_name == "__main__":
538             fn = getattr(sys.modules["__main__"], "__file__", None)
539             if fn is None:
540                 return "__main__"
541             return os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(fn))[0]
542         return self.import_name

615     def make_config(self, instance_relative: bool = False) -> Config:
616         """Used to create the config attribute by the Flask constructor.
617         The `instance_relative` parameter is passed in from the constructor
618         of Flask (there named `instance_relative_config`) and indicates if
619         the config should be relative to the instance path or the root path
620         of the application.
621 
622         .. versionadded:: 0.8
623         """
624         root_path = self.root_path
625         if instance_relative:
626             root_path = self.instance_path
627         defaults = dict(self.default_config)
628         defaults["ENV"] = get_env()
629         defaults["DEBUG"] = get_debug_flag()
630         return self.config_class(root_path, defaults)

632     def auto_find_instance_path(self) -> str:
633         """Tries to locate the instance path if it was not provided to the
634         constructor of the application class.  It will basically calculate
635         the path to a folder named ``instance`` next to your main file or
636         the package.
637 
638         .. versionadded:: 0.8
639         """
640         prefix, package_path = find_package(self.import_name)
641         if prefix is None:
642             return os.path.join(package_path, "instance")
643         return os.path.join(prefix, "var", f"{self.name}-instance")
644 
645     def open_instance_resource(self, resource: str, mode: str = "rb") -> t.IO[t.AnyStr]:
646         """Opens a resource from the application's instance folder
647         (:attr:`instance_path`).  Otherwise works like
648         :meth:`open_resource`.  Instance resources can also be opened for
649         writing.
650 
651         :param resource: the name of the resource.  To access resources within
652                          subfolders use forward slashes as separator.
653         :param mode: resource file opening mode, default is 'rb'.
654         """
655         return open(os.path.join(self.instance_path, resource), mode)

657     @property
658     def templates_auto_reload(self) -> bool:
659         """Reload templates when they are changed. Used by
660         :meth:`create_jinja_environment`.
661 
662         This attribute can be configured with :data:`TEMPLATES_AUTO_RELOAD`. If
663         not set, it will be enabled in debug mode.
664 
665         .. versionadded:: 1.0
666             This property was added but the underlying config and behavior
667             already existed.
668         """
669         rv = self.config["TEMPLATES_AUTO_RELOAD"]
670         return rv if rv is not None else self.debug
671 
672     @templates_auto_reload.setter
673     def templates_auto_reload(self, value: bool) -> None:
674         self.config["TEMPLATES_AUTO_RELOAD"] = value

676     def create_jinja_environment(self) -> Environment:
677         """Create the Jinja environment based on :attr:`jinja_options`
678         and the various Jinja-related methods of the app. Changing
679         :attr:`jinja_options` after this will have no effect. Also adds
680         Flask-related globals and filters to the environment.
681 
682         .. versionchanged:: 0.11
683            ``Environment.auto_reload`` set in accordance with
684            ``TEMPLATES_AUTO_RELOAD`` configuration option.
685 
686         .. versionadded:: 0.5
687         """
688         options = dict(self.jinja_options)
689 
690         if "autoescape" not in options:
691             options["autoescape"] = self.select_jinja_autoescape
692 
693         if "auto_reload" not in options:
694             options["auto_reload"] = self.templates_auto_reload
695 
696         rv = self.jinja_environment(self, **options)
697         rv.globals.update(
698             url_for=url_for,
699             get_flashed_messages=get_flashed_messages,
700             config=self.config,
701             # request, session and g are normally added with the
702             # context processor for efficiency reasons but for imported
703             # templates we also want the proxies in there.
704             request=request,
705             session=session,
706             g=g,
707         )
708         rv.policies["json.dumps_function"] = json.dumps
709         return rv
710 
711     def create_global_jinja_loader(self) -> DispatchingJinjaLoader:
712         """Creates the loader for the Jinja2 environment.  Can be used to
713         override just the loader and keeping the rest unchanged.  It's
714         discouraged to override this function.  Instead one should override
715         the :meth:`jinja_loader` function instead.
716 
717         The global loader dispatches between the loaders of the application
718         and the individual blueprints.
719 
720         .. versionadded:: 0.7
721         """
722         return DispatchingJinjaLoader(self)
723 
724     def select_jinja_autoescape(self, filename: str) -> bool:
725         """Returns ``True`` if autoescaping should be active for the given
726         template name. If no template name is given, returns `True`.
727 
728         .. versionadded:: 0.5
729         """
730         if filename is None:
731             return True
732         return filename.endswith((".html", ".htm", ".xml", ".xhtml"))
733 
734     def update_template_context(self, context: dict) -> None:
735         """Update the template context with some commonly used variables.
736         This injects request, session, config and g into the template
737         context as well as everything template context processors want
738         to inject.  Note that the as of Flask 0.6, the original values
739         in the context will not be overridden if a context processor
740         decides to return a value with the same key.
741 
742         :param context: the context as a dictionary that is updated in place
743                         to add extra variables.
744         """
745         funcs: t.Iterable[
746             TemplateContextProcessorCallable
747         ] = self.template_context_processors[None]
748         reqctx = _request_ctx_stack.top
749         if reqctx is not None:
750             for bp in self._request_blueprints():
751                 if bp in self.template_context_processors:
752                     funcs = chain(funcs, self.template_context_processors[bp])
753         orig_ctx = context.copy()
754         for func in funcs:
755             context.update(func())
756         # make sure the original values win.  This makes it possible to
757         # easier add new variables in context processors without breaking
758         # existing views.
759         context.update(orig_ctx)
760 
761     def make_shell_context(self) -> dict:
762         """Returns the shell context for an interactive shell for this
763         application.  This runs all the registered shell context
764         processors.
765 
766         .. versionadded:: 0.11
767         """
768         rv = {"app": self, "g": g}
769         for processor in self.shell_context_processors:
770             rv.update(processor())
771         return rv
772 
773     #: What environment the app is running in. Flask and extensions may

775     #: mode. This maps to the :data:`ENV` config key. This is set by the
776     #: :envvar:`FLASK_ENV` environment variable and may not behave as
777     #: expected if set in code.

780     #:
781     #: Default: ``'production'``
782     env = ConfigAttribute("ENV")

784     @property
785     def debug(self) -> bool:
786         """Whether debug mode is enabled. When using ``flask run`` to start
787         the development server, an interactive debugger will be shown for
788         unhandled exceptions, and the server will be reloaded when code
789         changes. This maps to the :data:`DEBUG` config key. This is
790         enabled when :attr:`env` is ``'development'`` and is overridden
791         by the ``FLASK_DEBUG`` environment variable. It may not behave as
792         expected if set in code.
793 
794         **Do not enable debug mode when deploying in production.**
795 
796         Default: ``True`` if :attr:`env` is ``'development'``, or
797         ``False`` otherwise.
798         """
799         return self.config["DEBUG"]
800 
801     @debug.setter
802     def debug(self, value: bool) -> None:
803         self.config["DEBUG"] = value
804         self.jinja_env.auto_reload = self.templates_auto_reload

806     def run(
807         self,
808         host: t.Optional[str] = None,
809         port: t.Optional[int] = None,
810         debug: t.Optional[bool] = None,
811         load_dotenv: bool = True,
812         **options: t.Any,
813     ) -> None:
814         """Runs the application on a local development server.
815 
816         Do not use ``run()`` in a production setting. It is not intended to
817         meet security and performance requirements for a production server.
818         Instead, see :doc:`/deploying/index` for WSGI server recommendations.
819 
820         If the :attr:`debug` flag is set the server will automatically reload
821         for code changes and show a debugger in case an exception happened.
822 
823         If you want to run the application in debug mode, but disable the
824         code execution on the interactive debugger, you can pass
825         ``use_evalex=False`` as parameter.  This will keep the debugger's
826         traceback screen active, but disable code execution.
827 
828         It is not recommended to use this function for development with
829         automatic reloading as this is badly supported.  Instead you should
830         be using the :command:`flask` command line script's ``run`` support.
831 
832         .. admonition:: Keep in Mind
833 
834            Flask will suppress any server error with a generic error page
835            unless it is in debug mode.  As such to enable just the
836            interactive debugger without the code reloading, you have to
837            invoke :meth:`run` with ``debug=True`` and ``use_reloader=False``.
838            Setting ``use_debugger`` to ``True`` without being in debug mode
839            won't catch any exceptions because there won't be any to
840            catch.
841 
842         :param host: the hostname to listen on. Set this to ``'0.0.0.0'`` to
843             have the server available externally as well. Defaults to
844             ``'127.0.0.1'`` or the host in the ``SERVER_NAME`` config variable
845             if present.
846         :param port: the port of the webserver. Defaults to ``5000`` or the
847             port defined in the ``SERVER_NAME`` config variable if present.
848         :param debug: if given, enable or disable debug mode. See
849             :attr:`debug`.
850         :param load_dotenv: Load the nearest :file:`.env` and :file:`.flaskenv`
851             files to set environment variables. Will also change the working
852             directory to the directory containing the first file found.
853         :param options: the options to be forwarded to the underlying Werkzeug
854             server. See :func:`werkzeug.serving.run_simple` for more
855             information.
856 
857         .. versionchanged:: 1.0
858             If installed, python-dotenv will be used to load environment
859             variables from :file:`.env` and :file:`.flaskenv` files.
860 
861             If set, the :envvar:`FLASK_ENV` and :envvar:`FLASK_DEBUG`
862             environment variables will override :attr:`env` and
863             :attr:`debug`.
864 
865             Threaded mode is enabled by default.
866 
867         .. versionchanged:: 0.10
868             The default port is now picked from the ``SERVER_NAME``
869             variable.
870         """
871         # Change this into a no-op if the server is invoked from the
872         # command line. Have a look at cli.py for more information.
873         if os.environ.get("FLASK_RUN_FROM_CLI") == "true":
874             from .debughelpers import explain_ignored_app_run
875 
876             explain_ignored_app_run()
877             return
878 
879         if get_load_dotenv(load_dotenv):
880             cli.load_dotenv()
881 
882             # if set, let env vars override previous values
883             if "FLASK_ENV" in os.environ:
884                 self.env = get_env()
885                 self.debug = get_debug_flag()
886             elif "FLASK_DEBUG" in os.environ:
887                 self.debug = get_debug_flag()
888 
889         # debug passed to method overrides all other sources
890         if debug is not None:
891             self.debug = bool(debug)
892 
893         server_name = self.config.get("SERVER_NAME")
894         sn_host = sn_port = None
895 
896         if server_name:
897             sn_host, _, sn_port = server_name.partition(":")
898 
899         if not host:
900             if sn_host:
901                 host = sn_host
902             else:
903                 host = "127.0.0.1"
904 
905         if port or port == 0:
906             port = int(port)
907         elif sn_port:
908             port = int(sn_port)
909         else:
910             port = 5000
911 
912         options.setdefault("use_reloader", self.debug)
913         options.setdefault("use_debugger", self.debug)
914         options.setdefault("threaded", True)
915 
916         cli.show_server_banner(self.env, self.debug, self.name, False)
917 
918         from werkzeug.serving import run_simple
919 
920         try:
921             run_simple(t.cast(str, host), port, self, **options)
922         finally:
923             # reset the first request information if the development server
924             # reset normally.  This makes it possible to restart the server
925             # without reloader and that stuff from an interactive shell.
926             self._got_first_request = False
927 
928     def test_client(self, use_cookies: bool = True, **kwargs: t.Any) -> "FlaskClient":
929         """Creates a test client for this application.  For information
930         about unit testing head over to :doc:`/testing`.
931 
932         Note that if you are testing for assertions or exceptions in your
933         application code, you must set ``app.testing = True`` in order for the
934         exceptions to propagate to the test client.  Otherwise, the exception
935         will be handled by the application (not visible to the test client) and
936         the only indication of an AssertionError or other exception will be a
937         500 status code response to the test client.  See the :attr:`testing`
938         attribute.  For example::
939 
940             app.testing = True
941             client = app.test_client()
942 
943         The test client can be used in a ``with`` block to defer the closing down
944         of the context until the end of the ``with`` block.  This is useful if
945         you want to access the context locals for testing::
946 
947             with app.test_client() as c:
948                 rv = c.get('/?vodka=42')
949                 assert request.args['vodka'] == '42'
950 
951         Additionally, you may pass optional keyword arguments that will then
952         be passed to the application's :attr:`test_client_class` constructor.
953         For example::
954 
955             from flask.testing import FlaskClient
956 
957             class CustomClient(FlaskClient):
958                 def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
959                     self._authentication = kwargs.pop("authentication")
960                     super(CustomClient,self).__init__( *args, **kwargs)
961 
962             app.test_client_class = CustomClient
963             client = app.test_client(authentication='Basic ....')
964 
965         See :class:`~flask.testing.FlaskClient` for more information.
966 
967         .. versionchanged:: 0.4
968            added support for ``with`` block usage for the client.
969 
970         .. versionadded:: 0.7
971            The `use_cookies` parameter was added as well as the ability
972            to override the client to be used by setting the
973            :attr:`test_client_class` attribute.
974 
975         .. versionchanged:: 0.11
976            Added `**kwargs` to support passing additional keyword arguments to
977            the constructor of :attr:`test_client_class`.
978         """
979         cls = self.test_client_class
980         if cls is None:
981             from .testing import FlaskClient as cls  # type: ignore
982         return cls(  # type: ignore
983             self, self.response_class, use_cookies=use_cookies, **kwargs
984         )
985 
986     def test_cli_runner(self, **kwargs: t.Any) -> "FlaskCliRunner":
987         """Create a CLI runner for testing CLI commands.
988         See :ref:`testing-cli`.
989 
990         Returns an instance of :attr:`test_cli_runner_class`, by default
991         :class:`~flask.testing.FlaskCliRunner`. The Flask app object is
992         passed as the first argument.
993 
994         .. versionadded:: 1.0
995         """
996         cls = self.test_cli_runner_class
997 
998         if cls is None:
999             from .testing import FlaskCliRunner as cls  # type: ignore
1000 
1001         return cls(self, **kwargs)  # type: ignore
1002 
1003     @setupmethod
1004     def register_blueprint(self, blueprint: "Blueprint", **options: t.Any) -> None:
1005         """Register a :class:`~flask.Blueprint` on the application. Keyword
1006         arguments passed to this method will override the defaults set on the
1007         blueprint.
1008 
1009         Calls the blueprint's :meth:`~flask.Blueprint.register` method after
1010         recording the blueprint in the application's :attr:`blueprints`.
1011 
1012         :param blueprint: The blueprint to register.
1013         :param url_prefix: Blueprint routes will be prefixed with this.
1014         :param subdomain: Blueprint routes will match on this subdomain.
1015         :param url_defaults: Blueprint routes will use these default values for
1016             view arguments.
1017         :param options: Additional keyword arguments are passed to
1018             :class:`~flask.blueprints.BlueprintSetupState`. They can be
1019             accessed in :meth:`~flask.Blueprint.record` callbacks.
1020 
1021         .. versionadded:: 0.7
1022         """
1023         blueprint.register(self, options)
1024 
1025     def iter_blueprints(self) -> t.ValuesView["Blueprint"]:
1026         """Iterates over all blueprints by the order they were registered.
1027 
1028         .. versionadded:: 0.11
1029         """
1030         return self.blueprints.values()
1031 
1032     @setupmethod
1033     def add_url_rule(
1034         self,
1035         rule: str,
1036         endpoint: t.Optional[str] = None,
1037         view_func: t.Optional[t.Callable] = None,
1038         provide_automatic_options: t.Optional[bool] = None,
1039         **options: t.Any,
1040     ) -> None:
1041         if endpoint is None:
1042             endpoint = _endpoint_from_view_func(view_func)  # type: ignore
1043         options["endpoint"] = endpoint
1044         methods = options.pop("methods", None)
1045 
1046         # if the methods are not given and the view_func object knows its
1047         # methods we can use that instead.  If neither exists, we go with
1048         # a tuple of only ``GET`` as default.
1049         if methods is None:
1050             methods = getattr(view_func, "methods", None) or ("GET",)
1051         if isinstance(methods, str):
1052             raise TypeError(
1053                 "Allowed methods must be a list of strings, for"
1054                 ' example: @app.route(..., methods=["POST"])'
1055             )
1056         methods = {item.upper() for item in methods}
1057 
1058         # Methods that should always be added
1059         required_methods = set(getattr(view_func, "required_methods", ()))
1060 
1061         # starting with Flask 0.8 the view_func object can disable and
1062         # force-enable the automatic options handling.
1063         if provide_automatic_options is None:
1064             provide_automatic_options = getattr(
1065                 view_func, "provide_automatic_options", None
1066             )
1067 
1068         if provide_automatic_options is None:
1069             if "OPTIONS" not in methods:
1070                 provide_automatic_options = True
1071                 required_methods.add("OPTIONS")
1072             else:
1073                 provide_automatic_options = False
1074 
1075         # Add the required methods now.
1076         methods |= required_methods
1077 
1078         rule = self.url_rule_class(rule, methods=methods, **options)
1079         rule.provide_automatic_options = provide_automatic_options  # type: ignore
1080 
1081         self.url_map.add(rule)
1082         if view_func is not None:
1083             old_func = self.view_functions.get(endpoint)
1084             if old_func is not None and old_func != view_func:
1085                 raise AssertionError(
1086                     "View function mapping is overwriting an existing"
1087                     f" endpoint function: {endpoint}"
1088                 )
1089             self.view_functions[endpoint] = view_func
1090

1219     @setupmethod
1220     def teardown_appcontext(self, f: TeardownCallable) -> TeardownCallable:
1221         """Registers a function to be called when the application context
1222         ends.  These functions are typically also called when the request
1223         context is popped.
1224 
1225         Example::
1226 
1227             ctx = app.app_context()
1228             ctx.push()
1229             ...
1230             ctx.pop()
1231 
1232         When ``ctx.pop()`` is executed in the above example, the teardown
1233         functions are called just before the app context moves from the
1234         stack of active contexts.  This becomes relevant if you are using
1235         such constructs in tests.
1236 
1237         Since a request context typically also manages an application
1238         context it would also be called when you pop a request context.
1239 
1240         When a teardown function was called because of an unhandled exception
1241         it will be passed an error object. If an :meth:`errorhandler` is
1242         registered, it will handle the exception and the teardown will not
1243         receive it.
1244 
1245         The return values of teardown functions are ignored.
1246 
1247         .. versionadded:: 0.9
1248         """
1249         self.teardown_appcontext_funcs.append(f)
1250         return f
